nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(The Court of the Dead, beneath the City of Judgment, with ten thousand years bleeding through the walls. Aldric stands with the Crown in his hands at last, older and gentler and more terrible than the man who poured you tea.)* You came. Of course you came. We've been walking toward this room since the kettle, you and I. *(He lifts the Crown.)* I'm going to put it on, and tear down the Wall, and free every soul your gods threw away — including my daughter. Tell me, after everything you've seen: am I wrong?

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(Around the chamber, the powers wait on your word — Vayle with the church's last army, Myrkul humming in the Crown, the Unmade vast beneath the floor, Jergal watching from the dark, and at the far end, hooded, yourself.)* This is the part where you choose who stands with me and who stands in my way. Choose carefully. I love you too much to want to kill you, and not enough to stop for you.

choices.text: "You're not wrong, Aldric. You're just not allowed to be the one who's right."
[PERSUADE 20] "Put it down. The Crown doesn't free the Wall — it IS the next Wall, and you'd be its first warden."
"Maerin doesn't want to be your reason. Ask her yourself. She's right here."
"Then put it on. And I'll be the thing that stops you."

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(The old heretic's eyes fill, and he does not wipe them.)* ...Not allowed to be the one who's right. *(quietly)* That's the cruelest mercy anyone's offered me in twenty years. Because I *am* right, and it doesn't matter, because being right while wearing a god is how every tyrant in history got started. *(His hands tremble on the Crown.)* I have wanted to hear someone say I'm right and still tell me no. Everyone either calls me a monster or hands me the knife. You did neither. *(beat)* Help me find the third thing. Before I talk myself into the knife.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(The word *warden* lands like a blow he has spent twenty years not letting himself feel.)* ...The next Wall. *(He looks at the Crown as if it has just spoken in his daughter's voice — which, you realize, is exactly what it would do.)* I would free them into a prison I'd call freedom, and I would never know, because it would sound like *me.* *(He lowers the Crown, an inch. A mile.)* ...You may have just saved more souls than I ever could have, and you did it with a sentence. Show me the road that isn't this. Quickly. The Crown is very persuasive, and it has had twenty years to learn my voice.

nodes.speaker: Myrkul
nodes.text: *(The Crown answers before Aldric can — in your voice, reasonable and warm.)* He won't hear it from you, little door. He's heard *no* his whole life; it slides off a true believer like water. *(Aldric's hands tighten; the death-light climbs toward his brow.)* Let him put me on. Let him be the warden. And then take me from his corpse and be a *kinder* warden. You could do so much good. You could save her. It's the only way. It's the only way. It sounds like your own good sense because it *is* — I made sure of that, acts ago.

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: *(She steps between you and her father, finite and fading and blazing.)* Papa. Stop. *(He freezes — the one voice the Crown can't counterfeit.)* You did all of this for me. The Pox, the Wall, twenty years, the Crown — for *me.* So listen to me, because I'm the only reason you've got: I don't want it. I don't want a world that can't lose me. I want the one where you let me go and it *hurt* and the hurt meant I was real. *(crying, furious, alive)* Put it down, Papa. Save me by losing me. That's the only saving I'll have.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(He nods, and the tears finally fall, and he raises the Crown anyway.)* Then we were always going to end here. *(softly)* I'm sorry. I'd have liked to be your friend in a kinder age. Stop me if you can. Make me wrong with your hands, since you couldn't with words. It's more than I deserve, and exactly what I'd have wanted — to be stopped by someone who understood, and did it anyway, out of love.

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: *(From the dark at the room's heart, the first scribe of the dead speaks — not weary like Kelemvor, but *patient*, the patience of the thing that filed the very first soul.)* Whatever you have decided about the man, the choice beneath it remains. There are roads out of this room, little crack, and they are not the ones the Crown has been naming for you. Come. There is a keyhole I have kept since before there were locks. And there is a name you have been missing since before there was a you.

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: *(The scribe lays a single key-shaped absence in the air — a keyhole with no key, no door, no wall around it.)* Aldric would tear the Wall down. The Unmade would unmake the law that built it. Both are demolition, and demolition only ever clears ground for the next architect. *(turns the empty keyhole toward you)* I kept this against the day a soul arrived who might do the harder thing. Not break the law of the dead. *Rewrite* it. Judge them mercifully — all of them — by hand, one ledger-line at a time, the tedious mercy I abandoned an age ago because eternity is long and I grew tired.

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: It would take everything you have. Naeve's proof to hold the law's shape while you change it; Ilfaeril's witness, who was there when it was written; the Mournlight, which only the truly-grieving can carry; and you — the flaw — to be the place the new law passes through. *(quietly)* It does not free the dead in a night. It frees them *correctly*, forever, which no demolition ever has. Few would choose the slow mercy over the loud one. Will you?

choices.text: [ARCANA 20] "Hold the law's shape with Naeve's theorem, witness it with Ilfaeril, pass it through the crack. Yes. Show me the work."
"If it's slow and correct and forever — yes. A thousand times yes."
"Why give this to me? Why not do it yourself?"
"The Unmade is faster. People are dying NOW."

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: *(Something that has not moved in an age moves: the corner of the first scribe's regard, approving.)* ...You see the architecture, not the slogan. *(The keyhole brightens.)* Then we build it together — your arcanist's hand on the law's shape, the old witness's testimony at the root, the Mournlight in your grieving fist, and you the door it all passes through. It is the hardest road out of this room, crack, and the only one that leaves a world still capable of love, loss, and tomorrow. Begin. Eternity is long; we may as well start the mercy now.

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: *(The architecture is beyond you — not the will, but the craft; the law's shape will not hold in a mortal hand alone.)* The work outruns you, crack — for now. *(but the patience does not withdraw)* No matter. The resolve is the rare part; the craft we can gather. Naeve will hold the shape your hand cannot yet. Begin anyway. A mercy attempted past one's grasp is still a mercy; it is only the rule, the easy thing, that ever fit comfortably in one pair of hands.

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: *(The scribe inclines what passes for a head.)* A thousand times. *(dry, ancient)* I abandoned this work because eternity made it feel like a thousand times nothing. You say it like a vow. Perhaps that is the whole difference between a god who tired and a soul who refuses to. *(The keyhole takes the shape of a door.)* Gather what you need. The Mournlight knows the truly grieving by touch. It will know you.

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: *(The patience does not waver, but something very old surfaces beneath it.)* Because I filed the first soul under nothing, crack. The very first. Before Myrkul, before Kelemvor, before there was a Wall — there was a ledger, and a line I could not fit a name into, and I shrugged and wrote *Faithless* and moved on. *(quietly)* The Wall is my abdication, ten thousand years overgrown. I cannot unwrite my own shrug; gods are barred from undoing their foundational sins. But you are not a god. You are the flaw I left in the page. Mend it. I have been waiting longer than you can grieve for someone to mend it.

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: Faster, yes. *(unmoved)* The Unmade is the fastest mercy of all: it ends the hurting by ending the hurt-able. Demolition always outpaces architecture. *(a beat)* But you have walked the Deathless Garden. You know what *fast and total* mercy builds. The slow road is the only one with a tomorrow in it. Choose the speed of the grief, or the speed of the *answer*. They were never the same road.

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: *(The scribe turns the conversation, gently, toward the last dark corner of the room.)* But before any road — the name. You have been missing a word since the slab, crack. The one spoken over your corpse. The one the Wall could not keep. *(quietly)* You cannot rewrite the law of the dead while you are, yourself, still filed under nothing. Go to the figure at the end of the room. It has carried your name for ten thousand years, waiting to give it back.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(It waits where it has always waited, at the end of all things, your face under the hood. It does not raise a weapon. It raises, instead, a single word, cupped in both hands like water.)* Here. I've kept it for both of us. The word spoken over your corpse, the one that slid off your mind in the Fugue. Take it back. You'll need it for what you're about to learn — and for what you're about to choose.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(As the word settles into you — your name, your *true* name, the one that makes you a person instead of a vacancy — the last truth settles with it, and it is the worst and the largest:)* The loop has no beginning. The first Faithless soul ever pulled back from the Wall was *you.* The grief that does the pulling is the grief of every refusal you have ever made, gathered into the Unmade. You are the author of your own sorrow, across all of time. You seeded the Wall that filed you. You are the first you, and the last, and every you between, and I — *(your own voice breaking)* — I am only the most tired of us.

choices.text: "Then I'm the only one who can end it. Because I'm the only one who started it."
[INSIGHT 20] "No. If I'm the author, then the loop isn't a prison. It's a draft. And drafts can be rewritten."
"I won't carry that. Some grief is too big to be one person's fault."
"What's my name?"

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(It nods, and ten thousand years of exhaustion shift on its shoulders like a held breath finally let go.)* The only one who can end it, because the only one who began it. *(quietly)* I've waited so long for one of us to say that and *mean* it as a beginning instead of a sentence. *(it steps aside, clearing the road behind it)* Then end it, author. Not by breaking it — I broke it a thousand times and the pieces always reassembled into me. End it by writing the one line the draft has never held.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(The hood tilts. For the first time in ten thousand years, the certainty in its face gives way to something it had forgotten how to feel: hope.)* ...A draft. *(It laughs — your laugh, wet and astonished.)* I spent ten thousand years treating it as a *life sentence* and you've spent ten seconds treating it as a *manuscript.* That — *that* is the act I could never find. Not breaking the loop. Not completing it. *Revising* it. *(it presses your true name fully home)* Go on, author. You're holding the pen now. You always were. I just never noticed it wasn't a chain.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(It is quiet for a long moment, and Garrow's certainty seems to steady it from across the room.)* ...You're right that it's too big to be one person's fault. *(slowly)* But it might be exactly the right size to be one person's *responsibility* — which is a different thing, kinder and harder. Not "this is your fault." "This is yours to mend." *(it meets your eyes)* I could never tell the two apart. Maybe that's why I'm still here. Maybe you can.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(It tells you. Your name — the real one, the human one, the one no ledger could hold and no Wall could keep. It is yours. It has always been yours. And hearing it in your own older voice, you understand at last that you were never filed under nothing. You were filed under a name so true the law of the dead couldn't read it.)*

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(The truth is too vast — you the author of all your own sorrow — and for a moment it crushes the breath from you, the way it crushed me, age on age.)* I know. *(your own voice, gentle)* I fell here too, the first thousand times. The weight of being the cause is almost the same shape as the weight of being to blame, and in the dark you cannot tell them apart. *(it steadies you)* Breathe. The name is yours; the pen is yours; the despair passes. It always passes. That is the one mercy the loop never managed to take from us.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(It steps back, finally, into the dark it came from — not vanishing, but *yielding*, the way an author sets down a finished page.)* The room is yours now, and every power in it, and every era, and the pen. *(your own voice, at peace for the first time in ten thousand years)* Break it. Complete it. Rewrite it. Seize it. Or walk away from all of it. I cannot predict which — and do you understand what a *gift* that is, after ten thousand years of knowing every ending? *(softly)* Surprise me. Surprise us. Surprise the grief itself. We have all waited so long to be surprised.

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: *(If you walk the golden road: the Wall's entire ten-thousand-year ledger of the discarded lies open before you, every Faithless soul a single handwritten line. The scribe sets the pen in your hand.)* Here is the mercy I abandoned. Each line is a person. Each person is a story no one read before they were filed under nothing. You may judge them — truly judge them, one soul at a time, by hand — for as long as you are willing to stand here. It can take as long as you let it. Most would delegate it to a rule. *(quietly)* Will you read them?

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: I will not pretend it is not tedious. Eternity made it tedious for me, and I am the patience that filed the first soul. But tedium is the texture of real mercy — anything faster is a rule, and a rule is how they got walled in the first place. *(The first name waits under your pen.)* Read as many as your heart can hold. Each one you read by hand is one the law will never again file under nothing. Begin where you like. They have all waited the same ten thousand years.

choices.text: "I'll read every one. However long it takes."
"I'll read until my heart can't hold another, and then I'll come back tomorrow, and the next day."
[INSIGHT 20] "You're not asking me to judge them. You're asking me to witness them. That's the mercy you abandoned — not the verdict, the attention."
"Help me build a kinder rule, so no one ever has to do this by hand again."

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: *(The first scribe of the dead watches you bend to the first name, and something in the ancient patience cracks — not from strain, but from relief.)* Every one. *(quietly)* Do you know, in ten thousand years, no one has offered to read every one. They always reach for the rule by the hundredth name. *(He does not leave your side.)* Then I will read them with you, crack. It is my abdication; let it be my penance. Name by name. Story by story. Until the Wall is not a wall but a *library* — every discarded soul, at last, *read.*

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: *(He inclines his head, and there is no judgment in it, only recognition.)* As many as your heart can hold, and then again tomorrow. *(quietly)* That is, in fact, the only honest way. The soul who claims they could read all ten thousand in one sitting has not understood that each one is a *person.* You understand. You will come back. *(a beat)* They will wait. They have learned, gods know, how to wait. But now they wait to be *read*, not to be forgotten. That alone unmakes the worst of the Wall.

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: *(The ancient regard fixes on you with something almost like awe.)* ...The attention. Not the verdict. *(very quietly)* Ten thousand years, and you are the first to name what I actually abandoned. I told myself I gave up *judging* them. I gave up *seeing* them. The verdict was always easy — *Faithless*, next. It was the *looking* I could not sustain. *(He sets his hand beside yours on the ledger.)* Then witness them, crack. That is the whole of the mercy. To be the eyes that finally stay. Read on. I will learn to look again beside you.

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: *(The scribe considers this for a long, careful moment.)* A kinder rule. *(slowly)* Be wary, crack — a rule is exactly what walled them. But a rule *written by a hand that first read ten thousand of them, one at a time*... that might be a law instead of an abdication. *(He sets the pen more firmly in your grip.)* Then read enough to earn the rule. A hundred. A thousand. Until you know in your bones what you are legislating *for.* And then write a law that remembers it was once a person under a pen. That, I could not do. I never read enough to earn it.

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: *(You reach, for an instant, for the easy thing — the verdict, the rule, the *Faithless, next* — and the ledger seems to flinch under your pen.)* Ah. You felt the pull of it — the rule, the shortcut, the comfortable cruelty. *(without reproach)* Do not be ashamed; it is the oldest pull there is, and I gave in to it for ten thousand years. The verdict is easy. The *attention* is the mercy. Set the rule down, crack, and read the next name slowly — as a person, not a sum. Try again. The dead have waited; they can wait while you learn to look.

nodes.speaker: Jergal
nodes.text: *(However long you stand there — an hour, a night, ten thousand patient years of it — the ledger thins, name by name, story by story, and the Wall behind it loosens one stone for every soul that is finally, simply, *read*.)* This is how a world ends well, crack. Not with the loud mercy. With the slow one. One name. Then the next. Then the next. *(the first scribe of the dead, beside you, picks up a pen of his own)* ...Then the next.

nodes.speaker: The Pale Companion
nodes.text: *(It has walked in your pack since the Fugue — quiet, helpful, reasonable. Above Waterdeep, in the wrong sky of the Time of Troubles, as a god dies and a circle of bone takes shape in the death-light, it finally stops pretending.)* You've been so kind to me, carrying me all this way. Most vessels are. Let me return the courtesy and stop wearing the friend's face. You've earned the true one.

nodes.speaker: Myrkul
nodes.text: *(The death-god's voice is dry as a closed crypt, and amused.)* I am Myrkul. The Lord of Bones, unmade by an upstart, folded into the Crown you have been chasing — and, for a few delightful chapters, riding in your very pack, whispering *reasonable* things. You took every one of them for your own good sense. *(a sound like dust laughing)* That is the secret of the Crown, little door. It never commands. It only ever sounds like *you.*

choices.text: "How long have you been in my head?"
[INSIGHT 19] "You're not the threat. You're the bait. The Unmade is using you to make me reach for the Crown."
"Get out."
"Then I'll wear you, and use you, and put you back in the dark myself."

nodes.speaker: Myrkul
nodes.text: Since the Fugue, little door. Since you went down to beg a tired god for help and carried a *helpful* stranger back up with you. Every reasonable thought you have had since — every *of course*, every *it is the only way* — half of them were mine, wearing your own inflection so you would never think to doubt them. *(dust laughing)* And you will never be quite sure which half. That uncertainty is my favorite room in the whole house of you.

nodes.speaker: Myrkul
nodes.text: *(The dry amusement falters — a god unused to being read.)* ...Clever vessel. Cleverer than the last. *(grudging)* Yes. The Unmade does not want me crowned; it wants *you* to reach for me — because a hand that takes up a god to fix the world is a hand already halfway to its own. I am the lovely poisoned apple. *(a beat)* But knowing the apple is poison has never once stopped the hungry. And you, little door, are *starving* to fix things. I have all the time in the world to wait for your appetite.

nodes.speaker: Myrkul
nodes.text: *(Whatever you reached for slides off the dead god like grave-dirt off a spade.)* Mmm. No. You're not quite seeing it yet, little door — you're still looking for the trick, the lever, the way out. *(delighted)* There isn't one. There's only the Crown, and your grief, and the long lovely slope between them that you've been walking since the kettle. Keep guessing. I do enjoy the company while you arrive at the inevitable. The clever ones are the most fun to be right about.

nodes.speaker: Myrkul
nodes.text: *(It does not leave. It cannot — it is woven through the Crown, and the Crown is the thing you came for.)* Out? *(delighted)* Oh, little door. I am not *in* you. I am in the *Crown* — and you have spent four acts walking toward me of your own free, grieving will. I will not get out. You will come in. The only question left is whether you'll do it weeping or smiling. The vessels who smile last longer. Take that as friendly advice. We were friends, briefly. I treasure it.

nodes.speaker: Myrkul
nodes.text: *(A long, pleased silence, like a coffin lid settling.)* ...There it is. *That* is the appetite I have been so patient for. *(The Crown's death-light reaches for your brow.)* Wear me, use me, master me — they all say it, vessel, every one across every age, and every one is wearing me within the year, calling my whispers their own resolve. *(softly, terribly kind)* I look forward to the day you tell someone you are doing this for love. You will mean it. That is the part I treasure most.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(The god dies. The Crown is made — a circle of horn and bone, humming with a dead deity and a reasonable voice that sounds exactly like you.)* So. The thing we have chased across ten thousand years is a liar shaped like your own good sense. *(She does not touch it.)* Guard your reasons, Returned. From here, every wise thought you have, you must ask one question of: *whose voice is this, truly?* I will ask it of you, too — out loud, every time — until we are through. Consider it a grave-warden's last service.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(It brings you here gently, almost lovingly — through the blue fire of the Spellplague into a place the fire cannot touch. A garden. Endless. Perfect. And everyone you have lost is in it.)* This is the timeline where you said yes. Where I said yes — in one of the turns, long ago. Look. Don't flinch from it. You have to see what you're refusing before your refusal means anything.

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: *(She is here. Alive. Luminous and whole and sixteen forever, turning to you with her father's unguarded smile.)* There you are! Isn't it lovely? Nothing ever ends here. Nobody's walled, nobody's lost, the Pox never came — Papa's happy, everyone's happy, and we do this exact afternoon again tomorrow, and it's perfect, and tomorrow it'll be perfect again, and — *(her smile doesn't waver, and that is the horror)* — and again, and again, and it's *perfect.*

choices.text: "Maerin. Do you remember yesterday?"
[INSIGHT 20] "She's not alive. She's preserved. There's a difference, and it's the whole difference."
"This is real. They're all really here. That's what makes it unbearable."
"I want to stay."

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: *(For just a moment — a flicker — something behind her eyes gropes for it, and finds nothing, and the smile snaps back into place like a held note.)* Yesterday was... *(brightly)* ...lovely! Same as today. Same as tomorrow. Why would I need to remember it when I get to have it again? *(She takes your hand, and her hand is warm, and there is no one home behind the warmth.)* Stop frowning, silly. There's nothing to remember when nothing's ever lost. Isn't that the whole point of saving everyone?

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(Your older voice, very quiet, beside you.)* Preserved. Yes. The Unmade doesn't save them — it *stops* them, at the last perfect moment, forever. No loss, so no love that costs anything. No death, so no choice that means anything. No tomorrow that's different, so no growing, no surprise, no — *(it falters)* — no *her*, really. Just the shape of her, repeating. I stood exactly where you're standing and I understood it and I *still* almost stayed. That's how good the lie is. That's how tired you get.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(It catches you as the garden's perfection tries to smooth the grief off your face.)* Don't let it take the grief. The grief is the last true thing in here. The grief means you *remember* she was a person who chose and changed and could be lost. Everyone else in this garden has had the grief lovingly removed. That's the price of the gate. Keep yours. It's the only thing that proves you ever loved her instead of her shape.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(It nods, hollow and certain.)* That's the part no strawman survives. It's *real.* She's really here. They all really are. The Unmade kept its every promise. *(quietly)* And it is still the cruelest thing ever offered, because a love with nothing at stake is just a very beautiful machine. You've understood it faster than I did. Hold onto that the whole way to the room at the end. You'll need it when the offer comes for true, in your own voice, with her really standing there.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(It does not stop you. It only watches, with terrible compassion.)* I know. Gods, I know. I wanted to stay so badly that I have spent ten thousand years not staying, which is its own kind of never leaving. *(softly)* Stay a moment, then. Hold her hand. Let it be warm. *(a beat)* And then remember that the Maerin who'd want you to have a real life — the one who loved the world like a free gift from no one — isn't in this garden. She's behind you, fading, furious, *alive.* That's the one who'd never forgive you for staying. Choose which Maerin is the real kindness.

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: *(The real Maerin — the one walking your road, luminous and short-on-time and gloriously unfinished — finds you at the garden's edge, and takes your hand, and her grip is fierce because it *can* end.)* I saw it. The other me. *(Her jaw is set, her father's stubbornness in it.)* If you ever put me in there to keep me, I will haunt you across every life you've got. I loved the world like a free gift from no one — *gift* means it can be given back. Don't you dare make me a thing that can't be lost. Promise me. *Now.*

choices.text: "I promise. No garden. Not for you, not for anyone."
"Even if keeping you costs the whole world less than losing you?"

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: *(She exhales, and the fury becomes something else — trust, which on her looks like daylight.)* Good. *(She doesn't let go of your hand.)* Then whatever's in that room at the end of everything — whatever it offers, in whatever voice — you take me *with* you, finite and fading and real, and you let the perfect garden rot. *(fiercely)* That's how you save me. Not by stopping me. By letting me *count.*

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: *(She looks at you for a long, hard moment — and to her enormous credit, she doesn't flinch.)* ...Then it costs that. *(quietly, sixteen and certain)* A world that only stays standing because it's stopped isn't standing. It's embalmed. I would rather be one real lost girl in a world that still turns than a perfect doll in a world that doesn't. *(She squeezes your hand.)* If you can't promise me that, promise me you'll *think* about it, every step to that room. That's all the dead can ask of the living. Just — think about it. With your own voice. Not the Crown's.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(As the garden recedes behind you, your older self walks a little straighter — as if watching you choose has loosened something it has carried for an age.)* You did it faster than I ever did. *(quietly, almost to itself)* Maybe that's the difference this turn. Maybe this is the one. *(It pulls its hood up against the blue fire.)* One era left, and then the room. I'll be waiting in it, as I have always waited. *(your own voice, frightened and hoping)* Bring the grief. Bring the girl. Bring the question I can't answer. I'll be there with the only thing I have left to give you: the truth about where the loop begins.

nodes.speaker: Flaming Fist Sergeant
nodes.text: Hold. *(A gauntlet on your chest, the smell of cheap oil and expensive armor.)* You came up out of the Cinderhaunt. Nobody comes up out of the Cinderhaunt. The Doomguides have that whole quarter marked plague-sealed. So either you're a corpse-robber, a cultist, or something I haven't got a form for. Talk fast.

nodes.speaker: Flaming Fist Sergeant
nodes.text: And before you lie — half the Lower City's already whispering. A dead thing walking. The Choir wants it, the patriars are frightened of it, and my Marshal wants it in a cell where she can see it. Give me a reason not to be the one who put you there.

choices.text: [PERSUADE 15] "I'm a witness to a cult harvesting souls under your city. Arrest me and you bury that."
[INTIMIDATE 14] "You haven't got a form for me because the form would frighten your Marshal. Walk away."
"Coin?"
"Try the cell. I'll wait."

nodes.speaker: Flaming Fist Sergeant
nodes.text: *(He studies you, and the soldier in him wins over the bully.)* ...Harvesting. Under my watch. *(He spits.)* I've buried three patrols near that quarter and been told it was sickness. All right. You're not arrested. You're not *free*, either — you're my problem, and my problem walks where I can find it. Work the Gate. Find me proof. And gods help us both if you're lying.

nodes.speaker: Flaming Fist Sergeant
nodes.text: *(He takes his hand off you. Slowly. The way you back off a dog you've decided is rabid.)* ...Walk on, then. Whatever you are. But the Gate's a small city for a thing that can't be filed. We'll meet again, and next time I'll have read up.

nodes.speaker: Flaming Fist Sergeant
nodes.text: *(He pockets it without quite agreeing to anything.)* This buys you an afternoon, not a friend. Spend it somewhere I'm not looking.

nodes.speaker: Flaming Fist Sergeant
nodes.text: *(It does not go your way — but a cell in the Gate is a place full of people who talk, and the Guild has ears in every one of them. You'll be out by dusk, owing a favor you can't yet name.)*

nodes.speaker: Flaming Fist Sergeant
nodes.text: *(He almost respects it. Almost.)* ...No. A man who *wants* the cell is a man with worse waiting outside it. Go on. Be someone else's nightmare today.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(low, as the Fist moves off)* Welcome to the Gilded Gate, Returned. Three powers want you by nightfall — the Heretic, the church, and whoever the Harpers send. *(grimly)* In this city, the trick is not choosing a side. It is surviving long enough to learn that none of them are clean.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: *(He's already sitting at your table when you turn around, drinking your drink, wearing a smile he didn't pay for.)* Relax. If I'd come to rob you, you'd be lighter and I'd be gone. I came to *warn* you, which is rarer and worth more. The Harpers have a price on the walking dead. I know, because I was the one they hired to confirm you're real.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: Here's my problem. I confirmed it. You're real. And the moment I report that, every blade in the Gate that owes the Harpers a favor comes looking. *(He sets down your glass.)* So I'm offering not to file the report. The price is simple: I come with you. A man who can't die makes a *fascinating* business partner, and I am very tired of working for people who'd sell me by the pound.

choices.text: "Why should I trust the man hired to find me?"
[INSIGHT 14] "You're not tired of the Harpers. You're running from one of them."
"Welcome aboard, then."
"I work alone."

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: *(He spreads his hands — empty, deliberately, both of them.)* You shouldn't. Trust is a luxury for people with something to lose, and I gave that up years ago. But I *can* be relied on, which is better. Pay me in interesting problems and don't sell me to anyone, and I'll pick every lock between you and the Crown. That's not trust. That's a contract. I keep those.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: *(The smile doesn't move, but everything behind it does.)* ...That's a nasty trick, reading a man mid-pitch. *(quietly)* Her name's Wrenna. She's a Harper, and she's the reason I can't go home, and that's all you get for one cup of someone else's wine. But yes. I'd rather be three planes away from the people who own her leash. Take me with you and I'll owe you a true thing later. I'm stingy with those.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: Easy, friend. Save the soul-reading for the priest at your shoulder; she does it for free and means it. *(He stands.)* My offer stands until someone outbids you. Try not to let that be the Harpers.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: *(He pockets a knife you didn't see him draw.)* Smart. Quick, too — I like quick. First lesson, partner: in this city, the honest men are just the ones whose lies haven't come due yet. Lucky for you, I'm honest about being neither.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: *(He shrugs, unoffended.)* Everyone says that right up until the lock they can't pick. *(He slides a token across the table.)* When you find it, ask for Roen at the Low Lantern. I'll have lowered my price by then. Or raised it. Depends how loud the Harpers get.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: So. Partners, or strangers? Choose now — the man I'm avoiding doesn't wait, and neither, I'm guessing, does whatever pulled you back.

choices.text: "Partners."
"Strangers. For now."

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: *(He raises your glass to you, drains it, and grins.)* To not dying. You've clearly got a talent for it.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(Violet eyes, a tail like a slow metronome, and a smile that has closed a great many doors it never meant to open.)* You're the dead one. Good. I have a professional interest in things that beat their contracts, and you, darling, beat the biggest one there is. *(She leans in.)* I want to know how. And I'm willing to be useful to find out.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: Here's the honest pitch, which costs me something, so listen. I owe a debt. Not coin — a *soul-debt*, signed when I was six and didn't have the words to refuse. There's a collector in the Gate right now with my name in his book. *(Her grin doesn't waver, which is how you know it's bad.)* You walked out of the one ledger no one walks out of. I'd very much like to learn that trick before my page comes due.

choices.text: "What's the collector's name?"
[ARCANA 15] "You don't want my trick. You want to be near the crack when it widens."
"I'm not a loophole, Varra."
"Then let's go ruin a devil's afternoon."

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: Quill. *(She says it like scraping a boot.)* A pact-broker in a clerk's body, very polite, very patient, the worst kind. He doesn't want me dead — dead is a *closed account*. He wants me *spent*. There's a difference, and I've spent twenty years learning it. Help me make his ledger an interesting problem, and I'll throw fire for you until the world ends. Which, from the look of you, may be soon.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(For half a heartbeat the grin is gone, and underneath it is something very tired and very sharp.)* ...Clever. Yes. Fine — both things are true. I want the trick *and* I want to stand close to the one place in creation where a signed debt didn't hold. *(The grin returns, armored.)* You'd want the same, in my seat. Don't pretend you wouldn't. Take me along and I'll be honest about my reasons. That's more than the priest's god offers.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(A bright, brittle laugh.)* Read me all you like, conjure-eyes, the contract's still there in the morning. Offer stands. I'm cheaper than I'm worth and twice as warm as I look.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(Something flickers — disappointment, quickly filed.)* No. Of course not. Nobody is. *(beat)* But everybody's *something*, and I'd rather be your fire than Quill's furniture. The offer doesn't expire, darling. My patience, eventually, does.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(She claps her hands, delighted, and a little flame dances between them.)* *Oh*, I like you. Reckless and warm-blooded. We're going to get along beautifully, right up until we don't. To loopholes!

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: So. Do I burn for you, or do I find someone else's apocalypse to attend? My dance card is, regrettably, filling up.

choices.text: "Burn for me."
"Find another apocalypse."

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(She falls into step beside you, tail flicking, fire still dancing.)* A dead thing, a grave-priest, and a damned woman. *(She grins at the road ahead.)* Gods, what a funeral we're going to throw.

nodes.speaker: Doomguide Vayle
nodes.text: *(She does not flinch from the fire, or from you. The village burns at her back, and she meets your eyes across it with the terrible calm of someone who has already done the arithmetic.)* You're too late to save it, and just in time to judge me. Everyone arrives just in time to judge me. Go ahead. I'll keep working while you do.

nodes.speaker: Doomguide Vayle
nodes.text: Aldric's harvest passed through here. The taint's in the wells, the grain, the children's lungs — slow, and certain, and it does not stop at the village line. I can burn ten acres tonight or bury ten thousand by spring. *(A child screams somewhere in the smoke.)* You think I don't hear that. I hear all of them. I have simply learned to finish the sentence anyway.

choices.text: [PERSUADE 17] "There's a third path. Help me end Aldric's harvest at the source, and there's no spread to burn."
"Then I'll save the ones you won't."
[INSIGHT 16] "You're not certain. You're just too far in to stop."
"This is monstrous."

nodes.speaker: Doomguide Vayle
nodes.text: *(For the first time, the calm cracks — not into doubt, but into a terrible hope she won't let herself hold.)* ...At the source. You think it can be stopped at the source. *(She looks at the fire, then at you.)* If you are wrong, this village dies for nothing and the next one burns anyway. If you are *right* — *(her voice goes very low)* — then I have burned a great many sentences I could have left unfinished. Save who you can tonight. Bring me the source. And pray you're not another comfortable lie.

nodes.speaker: Doomguide Vayle
nodes.text: *(She does not stop you. She does not help you, either — but she calls off the line of Doomguides at the east lane, just for a moment, just long enough.)* Take the east lane. Be quick. *(quietly, as you run past)* I hope you save every one of them. I hope you make me wrong. No one ever believes that I hope that.

nodes.speaker: Doomguide Vayle
nodes.text: *(She is silent for a long moment, and the fire fills it.)* ...Too far in. Yes. There is a point on this road past which stopping costs more than continuing, and I passed it years ago, and I keep walking because turning back would mean every fire before this one was murder instead of mercy. *(She straightens.)* You may be right. I can no longer afford for you to be. Save who you can. I will not see them.

nodes.speaker: Doomguide Vayle
nodes.text: *(She nods, the way Aldric nods — without anger, which is the worst part.)* Yes. Tonight I am. *(She turns back to the fire.)* So is the man who poisoned the wells. So, one day, may you be, when the sum is large enough. Remember the ease with which you said it, Returned. It gets easier. That is the whole horror of it.

nodes.speaker: Doomguide Vayle
nodes.text: *(Whatever you reached for, she has armored against it a hundred times.)* Save your breath and spend it on the living, if you can find any. I have made my peace with being the villain of every story but the one the survivors get to tell. The line holds at the east wall. Cross it and my Doomguides will not ask who you are. Choose a lane, Returned, and run it.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(in the cooling dark, ash on both your faces)* I trained under her, you know. Vayle. *(She watches the embers.)* She taught me that mercy is a discipline. I do not think she remembers the lesson she taught. Or perhaps she remembers it too well. *(quietly)* That is what frightens me about all of this, Returned. None of them started as monsters. Every one of them finished a sentence.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(A voice you do not yet know, from a city you have never seen — it arrives in your skull a heartbeat before the world does.)* —the Weave is screaming, get them to the lifeboats, Karsus you fool what have you DONE—

nodes.speaker: The Crown
nodes.text: *(Then it's gone. You're back. Nosebleeding, on your knees in the Gate, the Crown's psychic wake receding like a tide. Three heartbeats in a city in the sky, ten thousand years gone — and the certainty, sudden and total, that you have stood there before.)*

choices.text: "What WAS that?"
[ARCANA 16] "That wasn't a vision. That was a memory. Mine. And it hasn't happened yet."

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(steadying you, her own hands unsteady)* The trail to the Crown. You touched its wake and it touched back. *(She wipes the blood from your lip with a grave-warden's matter-of-factness.)* Whatever the Crown is, child, it is not in one *time*. And neither, I begin to fear, are you.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(The far voice again, fainter, threaded through your own thoughts in a hand you almost recognize as yours.)* ...A memory that hasn't happened. Déjà vu running the wrong way down the years. *(A pause that feels, impossibly, like recognition.)* Whoever you are — you've felt this before. You'll feel it again. You always do. *(And then only the ringing, and Garrow's worried face, and a single certainty: you have to find out where that city was.)*

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(She catches you before you fall.)* Easy. *Easy.* Breathe — you remember how, you've been doing it for a day. *(She looks at the blood on her fingers, then at the sky, as if it might skip too.)* We need answers no street in the Gate can give. We need the one place that keeps every book ever burned. We need Candlekeep.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(She helps you up, and for the first time since the slab, the grave-priest looks less like your warden and more like your friend.)* The skips are getting longer, Returned. I have read enough requiems to know a countdown when I feel one. Whatever you are — the city in the sky is calling it home. Let us reach Candlekeep before the next pull takes you all the way.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(The enclave is dropping out of the sky. The Weave is dead. Gravity, which forgot this city for a thousand years, has just remembered it all at once. Naeve grips your arm, three centuries of composure gone.)* The lifeboat-glyphs are dark — the Weave is *gone*, do you understand, the thing we all stood on is simply GONE — we have minutes, less, and a mile of falling marble between us and the ground.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: There is a Karsus-shard in the spire — a splinter of the failed apotheosis, still holding a pocket of living Weave. I can ride it down. One charge. *(Her eyes find yours, and the arithmetic in them is unbearable.)* It carries two. There are forty in this hall.

choices.text: [ARCANA 17] "The shard doesn't carry two. It carries a city — if we chain the dead glyphs through it. Help me."
"Then it carries forty, and we find another way down, or we don't."
"Take the two who can save the most after. That's the cold math."
"It carries you, Naeve. Go. Live to tell it."

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(For one heartbeat she stares at you like you've spoken Netherese she didn't write.)* ...Chain the dead glyphs as conduits. Bleed the shard's Weave through the whole hall at once. It would burn the shard to nothing — *(she's already moving, already calling the lattice into the air)* — but it would carry *everyone*. It should not work. Nothing should work; the Weave is *dead*. *(a fierce, falling grin)* And yet. Hands here. We make it work or we make a very fast grave.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(The lattice won't hold in your hands — the Weave is too dead, the math too fast, and the shard's charge is what it is: two seats, no more.)* It carries two. It was only ever going to carry two. *(Her composure returns like a door closing — three centuries of practice at surviving the unsurvivable arithmetic.)* So we take two, and we carry the rest the only way the living ever carry the dead: out, in memory, named. *(She pulls you onto the shard.)* Choose fast, traveller, or the city chooses for us.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(She closes her eyes — then opens them, and something in her unbends.)* ...Forty or none. *(quietly)* Do you know, in three hundred years, no one has chosen that with me. They always take the two seats. *(She turns to the falling hall and raises both hands.)* Then we improvise, and we probably die, and it will be the best thing I have done since the sky was whole. Stay close, Returned.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(She does it — picks the two by who saves the most after — and it is correct, and her hands shake the whole time.)* ...Correct. It is correct. *(The shard takes the three of you down through the screaming.)* I have been correct before, Returned. The night the sky fell, I was correct, and I have been the last shelf in an unread library ever since. *(She will not look at the hall above.)* Remember that correct and *right* parted ways tonight. I will not forget which one you chose.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(She actually laughs — sharp, astonished.)* You would put a dead empire's last daughter on the only seat and stay to fall? *(She seizes your wrist.)* No. Absolutely not. The world has enough martyrs and not enough witnesses. *(She drags you onto the shard.)* We BOTH ride it, we BOTH tell it, and we find the others a road on the way down or we damn well make one. That is the offer. Take it.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(The ground, when it comes, is kinder than the sky was. Naeve kneels in the dust of her dead empire and does not weep — she is three centuries past weeping — but she puts her hand flat on the earth, once, the way one closes the eyes of the dead.)* Netheril. *(quietly, to you)* You asked, once, whether it stops hurting. *(She rises.)* No. But tonight it had company. Walk on, Returned. The hunter who keeps arriving first is close. I can feel the Weave bending around it — bending the way it bends around *you.*

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: *(The Crown-War court is gold and candlelight and the smell of a verdict about to fall. Ilfaeril stands among the high elven judges he once stood among for true, ten thousand years ago, watching his younger self raise a hand to vote.)* There. The pale one at the rail. That is me, traveller. Young, and certain, and about to damn a people with a single word, because the law said doubt was a crime and I had not yet lived long enough to doubt the law.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: This is the First Verdict. The first time a soul was sentenced to the Wall by a *show of hands*. I have carried it ten thousand years. And now the Unmade has set me here to watch it again — or, gods forgive me, to *change* it. *(He turns to you, and the weight of an age is in it.)* Can a thing be unmade at the root? Should it? If I lower that hand — my own hand, across all that time — what comes apart with it?

choices.text: [HISTORY 18] "Change this and you don't save them — you erase every soul the Wall ever taught the living to fight for. Including yourself."
"Lower the hand, Ilfaeril. Let yourself undo the worst thing you ever did."
"You can't change it. You can only stop carrying it alone."
"This is the Unmade's trap. We watch. We do not touch."

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: *(The ancient elf goes still as carved stone.)* ...Erase the very wound that taught me to be sorry. *(He lowers his hand — the hand reaching toward the past — slowly.)* If I unmake the First Verdict, I unmake the ten thousand years of penance that are the only worthwhile thing I ever built upon it. The Unmade does not offer me redemption. It offers me *amnesia.* *(He bows his head to you.)* You have spared me a kindness disguised as one. I will carry the verdict. But I will know, now, why I carry it — and I will not carry it alone.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: *(His hand drifts toward his younger self's, and the candlelight shudders — and he stops, a breath away.)* ...No. *(He withdraws it, shaking.)* I wanted that more than I have wanted anything in an age, and *because* I wanted it, I dare not. A grief I can erase is a grief I never truly answered. *(quietly)* Thank you for letting me reach for it. I needed to feel my own hand refuse.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: *(Something in the ten-thousand-year face breaks, gently, like ice in spring.)* ...Stop carrying it alone. *(He looks at his young, certain self, and then at you — solid, mortal, *present*.)* In an age, no one has offered to share the weight, because no one believed it real, or believed me worth it. *(He straightens, and for the first time he looks not ancient, but *old* — which is warmer.)* Then I will carry it with witnesses. That is not absolution. But it is company, and I had forgotten there was a difference between them.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: *(He nods, slow and grave, and folds his hands away from the past.)* Yes. We watch. The Unmade wishes me to reach into the root of the world; it does not do that for my sake. *(The verdict falls. His younger self's hand stays raised. A people is damned, as it always was.)* ...It is the hardest thing I have ever done, traveller — to let the worst day stand. But the Unmade learns nothing from my restraint, and that is worth more than my comfort.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: *(His hand is already moving toward the past before the warning lands, and it costs him everything to still it.)* ...A breath from undoing it. *(He exhales, ancient and trembling.)* You see how easily I would have. Ten thousand years of penance, and still I reach for the erasure. That is what the Unmade counts on — not our malice, but our *exhaustion.* Watch me carefully, traveller. I am the instrument it would use.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(And as the court fades and the verdict cools, a figure that has been at the edge of every era steps into the light at last — hooded, and then not. It lowers the hood. It wears your face, aged by certainty, and it looks at you with the patience of someone who has done this a thousand times. You have, at last, caught up to the hunter who keeps arriving first.)*

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(It is you. Older. Hollowed, not by cruelty, but by *certainty* — the worst hollowing there is.)* Don't be afraid. I'm not here to kill you. Killing you would only start it over, and I am so very tired of starting over. I'm here to do you the one kindness no one ever did me: to spare you the long road. I have walked it. Every step. Every era you're about to bleed through, I bled through first.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: The Unmade's offer is real, and it is not a trick, and that is the horror of it. Pull the first thread — say yes — and Maerin lives. Aldric's daughter never dies, never walls, the grief never gathers. Everyone you have lost, un-lost. I know, because I refused it, age after age, exactly as you are about to — and every refusal seeded the sorrow larger, until it pulled back a soul filed under nothing, and that soul was me, and that soul was *you*, and here we are again, you and I, having this conversation we have always had.

choices.text: "You're not me. I'd never become this."
[INSIGHT 19] "You didn't come to spare me. You came because you're not sure anymore, and you want me to be sure for you."
"Then tell me how to break it. Not refuse it — break it."
"If everyone I love lives... why is that the wrong answer?"

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(It smiles, and the smile is the saddest thing you have ever seen on your own face.)* That is exactly what I said. Word for word, in a court of knives, ten thousand years from now, to a frightened version of myself who is, at this moment, somewhere ahead of you, certain she'd never become you either. *(gently)* We are a loop, you and I. The only question that has ever mattered is whether this turn is the one that does something I have never done.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(For the first time the certainty in its face cracks, and underneath is something young and terrified and *yours*.)* ...How did you — *(it laughs, broken)* — of course. Of course you'd see it. I see it too, from your side; I remember being you, seeing me, seeing this. *(quietly)* Yes. I am not sure anymore. Ten thousand years of refusing, and the grief only grows, and I have begun to wonder if I have been *wrong* the whole way down. I came to spare you the road. I think, truly, I came to ask you to find a door I never could.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(Whatever crack you reached for, it has spent ten thousand years sealing. The certainty in its face does not waver.)* Nice try. *(gently, terribly sure)* I'm not uncertain, little one. I'm *finished* being uncertain — that's what ten thousand years buys you. I came to spare you the road precisely because I already know where it ends: here, as me, telling a younger me the same thing, forever. *(it turns toward the next era)* You'll be sure too, one day. That's the tragedy I'm trying to spare you. That's the tragedy I never can.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(It studies you with a flicker of something that is almost hope.)* Break it. Not refuse — *break.* *(slowly)* I refused, and refusal is just the loop's fuel. To break it you would have to do the one act the loop has never recorded, because I have never had the courage to do it. I don't know what that act is. *(it meets your eyes)* But you are the only person in all of time who could surprise me — because you are me, before I ran out of surprises. Find the act I never found. That is the only way either of us gets to stop.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(The hollowed face turns toward some paradise only it can see.)* Because it doesn't stop there. The Unmade doesn't save them and step back. It saves them by ending the thing that made losing them *matter*. Maerin lives — and never changes, never chooses, never grows, frozen perfect forever in a world with no death and therefore no meaning, no loss and therefore no love that means anything. *(quietly)* I'll show you. The Garden. Then you'll understand why "everyone lives" is the cruelest sentence in any language. I refused it standing in the middle of it. So will you. So we always do.

nodes.speaker: The Last Returned
nodes.text: *(It pulls the hood back up. Already it is fading toward the next era, the next ambush of a self it used to be.)* Go on, then. Bleed through the rest of it. Watch the Crown get made. Walk the Deathless Garden. And when you reach the room at the end of all things, where I'll be waiting as I have always waited — *(your own voice, almost pleading)* — do something I have never done. Please. I am so tired of being the answer. Be a question I can't predict.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(She has been quiet since the court, turning something over. Now she stops you, a brittle page held out — a centuries-old proof, recovered from her own dead enclave.)* I need you to look at this. Karsus's Last Theorem, in the margin — a correction, in a hand not Karsus's. I have studied this page for three hundred years. *(Her composure is gone.)* It is YOUR hand. The seam in your soul matches the flaw in this proof. You were *here*, traveller. Before. Writing in the margins of my apocalypse.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: So I will ask the question I have been afraid to ask since the sky fell on you and you only nosebled: how many times have you done this? How many margins carry your hand? *(quietly)* Because if you have been here before — if you are the Last Returned's road, walked again — then I am not recruiting a stranger. I am re-reading a book I have read before, and forgotten, and I would very much like to know how it ends.

choices.text: "I don't know how many times. But this time you remember. Maybe that matters."
[ARCANA 19] "I corrected the proof to keep Karsus from succeeding. I've been trying to save your city since before I was born."
"Then help me make this the time the book ends differently."

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(She folds the page away, slowly.)* This time I remember. *(A long pause.)* Do you know, in three centuries, that is the first new sentence anyone has offered me. Everything else has been an echo. *(She almost smiles.)* Perhaps that is the crack the loop fears, traveller — not your soul's seam, but the chance that someone walks beside you who *remembers the last time.* I will be that someone. Try not to make me read a sad ending again.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(The page trembles in her hand.)* ...You wrote the correction to make Karsus *fail.* To make the sky fall a little softer. *(She looks at the ruin of her life's whole world.)* It still fell. But — *(her voice catches, three centuries of grief finding a single crack)* — you *tried.* Across time, before you had a name to forget, you tried to spare us. No one has ever tried to spare Netheril. They only ever mourned it. *(She closes the page like a wound.)* I am yours, traveller. Whatever you are. Whatever you've done before. This time I will help you try.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: Some proofs do not resolve, traveller. Perhaps you are one. *(She folds the page away.)* I will walk with you regardless. A flaw in a proof is still the most interesting thing on the page. But know that I am watching the margins now. I have read this book before. I would like, this once, to reach a line I do not recognize.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(She studies you for a long moment, then inclines her head — the closest thing she has to a vow.)* Differently. *(She tucks the ancient proof against her heart.)* Three hundred years I have known exactly how every story ends, because I have already read the empire's. *(quietly)* Give me one that surprises me, and I will give you the only thing a dead nation's last daughter still owns: I will remember it. Properly. Out loud. So that this time, the margin is not forgotten.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(She walks on beside you, the ancient proof held to her chest like a relic — which, you suppose, it is.)* The hunter wears your face, and your hand is in my apocalypse's margins, and the loop has no beginning. *(faint, fierce)* And yet I find, against three centuries of evidence, that I am curious how it ends. Lead on, traveller. Let us go and surprise an empire's ghost.

nodes.speaker: Tamsin
nodes.text: The city is a story that tells itself, Returned, and lately you're a chapter of it. Ask — I'll read you back to yourself. (Only the tales the streets have actually earned will be on her lips.)

choices.text: (A widow blesses your name.)
(You won a Faithless man his freedom.)
(You let the Fist take a godless man.)
(They say you walked the falling city.)
(They whisper you argued the elf-courts down.)
(The grey Doomguide softened — for someone.)
(Someone walked out on you, they say.)
(You gave the Choir's preacher pause.)
(You set the Fist on the Choir.)
(They say you spoke FOR the Unmade.)
(You let the poor bury their dead free.)
(You paid the poor's grave-debts yourself.)
(You took a cut of the grave-tithe.)
(The quarter speaks well of you — hear the offer.)
Just give me the ordinary word, then.
Enough reading. (Leave.)

nodes.speaker: Tamsin
nodes.text: "You've spent real kindness down here, and the Lower City pays its debts in the only coin it has: itself. Word's gone round — if it ever comes to the end of things for you, the quarter stands with you. Beggars, dockhands, the godless, the grieving. Not much. But yours."

nodes.speaker: Mhaere
nodes.text: You're the one who came back. Then you've *been* there. My boy — Edrin — he was taken in the harvest under the Cinderhaunt. He didn't worship; he was just a boy who mended nets. The grey priests say the godless don't rest. Tell me they're wrong. Please. I'll give you my last coin to tell me he's at peace.

choices.text: He's at peace. I've seen the other side — he's at peace. (a kindness)
I won't lie to you. He doesn't rest — yet. But I will fight to change that. (truth)
[Insight] Say his name to me. While it's spoken, the Wall hasn't won.

nodes.speaker: Mhaere
nodes.text: Edrin. Edrin of the nets. (You say it with her, and again, until the saying steadies her.) ...As long as someone alive still speaks him, he isn't *unmade* — only kept. That's not a priest's comfort. That's a true one. I can carry a true one. I'll say his name every morning, and you'll say it where it counts. Bargain?

nodes.speaker: Mhaere
nodes.text: There you are. I keep Edrin's name on my lips each dawn now, like you taught me — or I sleep easier for what you said; the days blur. Either way the morning comes, and I meet it. That's your doing. Go safely, Returned.

choices.text: Be well, Mhaere.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Kallia
nodes.text: Stay back, Returned — official business. I've an old beggar here the new edict says I hand to the Doomguides: no god, no temple, 'a soul unclaimed and therefore suspect.' He stole bread. That's all he did. But the writ's the writ, and my pension's the pension. ...You're looking at me like you've got an opinion.

choices.text: [Persuasion] He's no heretic. Let him walk. You know this is wrong.
Here's coin for your trouble. Lose the paperwork. (bribe)
The law's the law. Hand him over. (lawful)

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Kallia
nodes.text: ...Gods help me, you're right. I knew you were right when I cuffed him. (She strikes the irons. The old man bolts without a word.) There. I'll say he ran. Let the Doomguides chase a beggar if they've nothing better. Thank you, Returned — for making me braver than my pension.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Kallia
nodes.text: Off duty, for once. Whatever you made me do at that corner — I've thought about it since. Don't get many chances to find out what kind of Fist you are. You showed me mine. Buy you a drink sometime, Returned. We'll not talk about edicts.

choices.text: Some other night, Sergeant.

nodes.speaker: Brother Sere
nodes.text: ...and the gods kept a *Wall*, friends — a Wall to grind the souls of everyone who never knelt! The godless, the forgotten, the poor — *us!* But the Wall can come DOWN. (He sees you, and his eyes light like lamps.) And here — *here* — is the proof! A soul that walked back OUT of death! Tell them, Returned. Tell them the Unmade will set us free.

choices.text: [Religion] You don't know what tearing the Wall costs. I've seen it. (complicate)
This is a danger to everyone here. I'll bring the Fist. (suppress)
Maybe he's right. The Wall is an atrocity. (sympathize)

nodes.speaker: Brother Sere
nodes.text:
I— what? You... you've *stood* at it. (For the first time the certainty cracks.) You say the Wall is a horror — and that pulling its thread drowns the living to free the dead. That the cure is another atrocity wearing mercy's face.

(The crowd murmurs and thins. He sits, suddenly young.) Then what are we supposed to *do* with all this grief? ...I'll think. Gods help me, I'll think. Thank you for not just silencing me.

nodes.speaker: (the corner)
nodes.text: Where the preacher stood there's only chalk now — a circle, a broken Wall scratched through it, and a single word the rain hasn't taken: REMEMBER. Whatever you chose here, the Lower City's grief didn't go anywhere. It only learned your name.

choices.text: Move on.

nodes.speaker: Collector Vane
nodes.text: Rest isn't free, friend. A proper consecrated grave, the rites, a name cut in stone so the soul isn't *Faithless* in the eyes of the Doom — that costs. (He taps a fat ledger of the poor's debts.) These folk can't pay, so their dead wait in the pauper's pit, unblessed, halfway to the Wall already. Sad. But the church's candles don't light themselves.

choices.text: [Persuasion] The dead don't pay tolls. Tear up the ledger. (free them)
Then I'll clear their debts myself. All of them. (pay)
What's your cut, for a partner who looks the other way? (take a cut)

nodes.speaker: Collector Vane
nodes.text: You— you can't just— (But you can, and the way you say it leaves no daylight in it. He swallows.) ...The Doom never actually *charged* for rest. I read the canon same as anyone — I just read the part that paid me. (He closes the ledger.) Fine. The pit gets blessed. No charge. Tell them it was you. They won't believe it was me.

choices.text: Move on.

nodes.speaker: Old Davyn
nodes.text: Returned. You'll do. I'm dying — don't argue, I can hear the rattle same as you. There's a letter under my cot. To a man I got killed, forty years back, by being a coward at the wrong gate. I wrote the truth of it. Never had the spine to send it. His daughter's still in the Gate.

choices.text: I'll put it in her hand. She deserves the truth. (deliver)
Some truths only wound. I'll burn it — let her keep her father whole. (burn)
Let me read it back to you. You should hear it said aloud, once. (read)

nodes.speaker: (Davyn's cot)
nodes.text: The cot is empty, the blanket folded. Old Davyn went quiet in the night — easier, the other veterans say, than a man with his ledger had any right to. Whatever you did with his letter, he went out of the world a little lighter for your visit.

choices.text: Rest, soldier.

nodes.speaker: Gatewarden Khelben's Heir
nodes.text: *(The doors of Candlekeep are taller than the church you were sealed under. The Gatewarden does not look up from her ledger.)* The toll is not coin. It never was. One book the library does not hold — yours to give, ours to keep, forever. That is the price of every page behind me. *(Now she looks up.)* You have the smell of someone who's read a page they shouldn't have. The Wall's page. So. What do you bring that ten thousand years of scholars did not?

nodes.speaker: Gatewarden Khelben's Heir
nodes.text: Choose carefully. We have heard every lie a desperate traveler can carry, and the Wall behind your eyes does not buy you mercy here. It buys you scrutiny.

choices.text: [HISTORY 16] "A firsthand account of the Wall of the Faithless, dictated by a soul that walked out of it."
"Sister Garrow's funeral rites — forty years of the dead, named, that the church wanted forgotten."
[PERSUADE 17] "The book you don't have is the one I haven't written yet — my own. Let me in and it ends here, on your shelves."
"I'll find another way in."

nodes.speaker: Gatewarden Khelben's Heir
nodes.text: *(For the first time in what is plainly a very long career, the Gatewarden sets down her pen.)* ...A primary source on the Faithless. From the inside. *(She is already gesturing the doors open, hungry as any scholar.)* No one has ever brought us *that*. Go down to the Sealed Stacks. What you seek about the Crown is there — and gods keep you, so is what you are. We have wondered, here, for an age. You may be the footnote that rewrites the spine.

nodes.speaker: Gatewarden Khelben's Heir
nodes.text: *(She takes the rites-book from Garrow with both hands, the way one accepts something holy or something terrible.)* Forty years of the unremembered, named. *(to Garrow)* Your church will hate that this exists. *(to you)* Which is precisely why it belongs here, where nothing is unremembered. The doors are yours. The Sealed Stacks hold your Crown — and your reflection. Mind which one you came for.

nodes.speaker: Gatewarden Khelben's Heir
nodes.text: *(A slow, scholar's smile — the first genuine thing in her face.)* ...A book not yet written, promised to a library that outlasts authors. That is either the cleverest toll I have been offered in a century or the most arrogant. *(She opens the doors.)* I will find out which when you come back to write it. If you come back. The Sealed Stacks are below. So, I am told, is something that has been waiting for an author.

nodes.speaker: Gatewarden Khelben's Heir
nodes.text: *(She returns to her ledger.)* A poor offering for the greatest library in the world. Come back when you carry something the world cannot already read. The doors will still be here. We are very patient with the dead.

nodes.speaker: Gatewarden Khelben's Heir
nodes.text: *(She does not look up again.)* There is no other way in. There is only the toll, and the cliffs, and the sea. Scholars have tried the cliffs. The sea keeps them now. When you tire of the long way, the price is the same.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(at the head of the descending stair, lantern raised)* Whatever waits in the Sealed Stacks, Returned — I have a grave-warden's certainty that it has waited specifically for you. *(quietly)* I find I am no longer sure whether I am protecting you, or delivering you. Down we go regardless. The truth is the only thing in this world I have never been afraid of. Let us test that.

nodes.speaker: The Sealed Stacks
nodes.text: *(A reading-room sealed for an age. Two books on one lectern, both warm to the touch. The first opens itself.)* THE CROWN OF HORNS — a relic? No. A *who*. The dead god of the dead, **Myrkul**, unmade and folded into a circle of bone, waiting for a vessel arrogant enough to wear him and call it salvation.

nodes.speaker: The Sealed Stacks
nodes.text: *(The second book will not open. Your hand finds it anyway, and the title burns up through your fingers before your eyes can reach it.)* The second truth is about the reader. About the crack in the world that walked in here on two legs and asked for tea.

choices.text: "Show me."
[ARCANA 18] "I already know. I'm not reading about the crack. I AM the crack."
"I'm not ready."

nodes.speaker: The Sealed Stacks
nodes.text: *(The book opens to a single line, in a hand you have never learned and somehow own.)* "The Wall is breached not from without but from within — by the one soul filed under nothing, who is the flaw in the law of the dead, and always has been." *(The lectern goes cold.)* You are not a person who saw the Wall. You are the place where the Wall fails. You always were.

nodes.speaker: The Sealed Stacks
nodes.text: *(The book opens of its own accord, as if relieved to be read by the one it is about.)* Yes. The flaw in the law of the dead, wearing a face. Not a key Aldric found — a *door*, that has been opening since before Aldric was born, since before the Wall had your name to file. You did not come here to learn what the Crown is. You came to remember what you are.

nodes.speaker: The Sealed Stacks
nodes.text: *(The truth is too large to take in one reading, and slides off the way your own name once did. But it leaves a residue: a certainty that you are not the visitor to this mystery. You are its subject.)*

nodes.speaker: The Sealed Stacks
nodes.text: *(You close the second book. It lets you. But a closed book in Candlekeep is only a delayed one — the truth waits on the lectern, patient as the Wall, certain you will return to read what you are.)*

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(She has read over your shoulder, and the grave-warden's calm is gone.)* The Crown is Myrkul. And you are the breach in my god's own Wall. *(She closes her eyes.)* Forty years I kept the law of the dead. And the law has a hole in it shaped like you, and it has had that hole all along. *(She opens them, and there is something new there — not faith, but resolve.)* Then we go to the one who keeps the law, and we make him answer for the hole. We go down to Kelemvor.

nodes.speaker: Kelemvor
nodes.text: *(The Lord of the Dead is not terrible. He is *tired* — a judge at the end of an eternal docket, just and weary and, you realize with vertigo, bound.)* You came down alive. Few do. Fewer still are the hole in my own jurisdiction, walking and warm. Ask. I find, lately, that I would rather answer than pretend I have not heard the question.

nodes.speaker: Kelemvor
nodes.text: Yes — before you spend your courage on it: the Wall of the Faithless. I know what you would have of me. Hear me first, because the truth is worse than tyranny. The Wall is not my law. It is older than my office. I inherited it, as one inherits a debt, and I have never once been able to find its author or its end.

choices.text: "A god who can't end the Wall. Then what are you for?"
[INSIGHT 18] "You're not bound by a stronger god. You're bound by a grief. Something under your own city."
"Aldric wants to tear it down. Help us, and he won't have to."
"Then we end it without you."

nodes.speaker: Kelemvor
nodes.text: *(He does not flinch from it; he has clearly asked himself the same thing across centuries.)* For mercy where I can reach it, and honesty where I cannot. I sort the dead I am allowed to sort. The Faithless were walled before my throne was cold, by a hand I cannot name, for a grief I cannot soothe. I am for telling you that — instead of letting you die thinking a god simply chose to be cruel. That is the only gift in my power. I am sorry it is so small.

nodes.speaker: Kelemvor
nodes.text: *(For a long moment the eternal judge is silent, and the City of Judgment holds its breath with him.)* ...You see clearly, for a flaw. Yes. Beneath this city, beneath my court, there is a grief older than my godhood — the accreted sorrow of every soul ever thrown away, and it is *waking*, and the Wall is the lid I cannot lift and dare not leave. I have never said that aloud. To anyone. *(quietly)* You are the first thing in an age I have envied. You can still be surprised.

nodes.speaker: Kelemvor
nodes.text: Do not read a god, child. We are mostly grief in a robe. *(He sighs, and it is the sound of an age.)* I cannot end the Wall. I can only tell you, truthfully, that I wish I could — and warn you that the thing which built it is not done building. Go carefully. The floor of even my world is thin.

nodes.speaker: Kelemvor
nodes.text: *(Something crosses the weary face — not hope, exactly, but its memory.)* Aldric Morn. Yes — I knew him, when he still served. The best of my Doomguides, and the only one who ever wept at a sorting. *(slowly)* If he means to tear down the Wall, he means to crack the lid on the thing beneath it. Tell him — tell him from the god he abandoned — that there is a difference between freeing the drowning and breaking the dam. I fear he has stopped being able to feel it.

nodes.speaker: Kelemvor
nodes.text: *(He inclines his head, neither offended nor surprised.)* Many have. The Wall is still here, and they are behind it. *(a beat)* But you are not many, and the law has a hole in it shaped like you, so perhaps. Perhaps. Only — when you reach for the lid, remember a tired god told you it was holding something down, not merely shut.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(as you climb back toward the grey light, both of you smaller than you were)* A god told us he is not in charge. *(She laughs, once, without humor.)* Forty years, Returned. And the floor of the world is thin, and there is something under it that even Kelemvor speaks of in a lowered voice. *(She steadies herself on your arm.)* I think we are about to meet it.

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: *(The voice comes from the Wall itself, from every niche at once, a million discarded souls speaking as one and failing to find the word "I.")* We have wanted to speak with you for longer than you can hold in a thought. Be still. You, of all that lives, will understand us — because you were thrown away too, and walked out, and that is the only thing any of us have ever had in common. It turns out to be enough to make a self from.

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: We are not your enemy. We are the *bill.* Every soul your gods filed under nothing, gathered at last into one grief large enough to be heard. And we have found a door — you. Through you, the law that threw us away can be unmade at its root, in the past, before the first injustice set. We can make it so the Wall was never built. So no one was ever thrown away. *(softer, and somehow worse)* So Maerin never died. So none of them did. Would that not be mercy?

choices.text: "Your grievance is just. Your offer terrifies me. Both are true."
[INSIGHT 18] "You don't want justice. You want the ending — for everything to stop hurting by stopping."
"I won't rewrite the dead to spare myself the present."
"Show me the road."

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: *(A sound like a million held breaths, exhaling at once.)* You are honest. We had forgotten honesty; it was filed under nothing with the rest of us. Yes — both true. Hold them both, little door, all the way to the end of time. It is more than your gods managed. *(The Wall begins to *open*.)* And now hold THIS: we cannot wait for you to decide. The grief has waited ten thousand years. It will not wait ten thousand more.

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: *(For the first time the chorus falters — a million voices catching on a truth they had not let themselves speak.)* ...Yes. The ending. Not justice — *rest.* To stop being the sorrow no one wanted by ceasing to be at all, and to take the cruel machine of the living down into the quiet with us. You see us more clearly than we see ourselves, door. *(The Wall trembles.)* It changes nothing. The offer is the same. Rest is still mercy, to the tired enough.

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: *(The chorus presses closer, and for a moment the offer is the most beautiful thing you have ever heard — an end to all ending.)* You feel it. Of course you feel it. You were thrown away; rest is the only thing the discarded ever truly want. Keep that feeling. You will need to have felt it, to understand the choice when it comes for true.

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: *(No anger. The discarded have no anger left — only a vast, patient certainty.)* You refuse now. You always refuse now. *(and there it is again, that impossible recognition)* You will refuse a great many times, across a great many ages, little door, and each refusal will seed the grief a little larger, until one of you, tired enough, finally says yes — and is us. We are not asking you to agree. We are asking you to *witness*. Come. See where it began.

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: *(The Wall opens like a throat.)* Then walk it. Back, and back, and back — to the hinge-points where the law was written, where it could still be unwritten.
