We will scatter you across them. Survive them. Learn us. And when you have seen it all, choose — knowing, for once, exactly what you are choosing.

nodes.speaker: The Unmade
nodes.text: *(And then the floor of 1486 is gone. The pull behind your navel — the one that dragged you off the slab — seizes you whole, and the grey court tears away, and Garrow's hand closes on empty air where you were. You fall up the years. You fall ten thousand of them. And somewhere below, getting larger, is a city in the sky on the worst day in the history of magic.)*

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: Sit. The tea's real — Calishite, smuggled, scandalous. That's rarer than honesty in this city, so I lead with it. You're the one who came back. I've wanted to meet you more than I've wanted anything in a long while.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: You know what I am — the Doomguides have papered the Gate with my face and the word HERETIC beneath it. So let me tell you the truth they won't: I mean to tear down the Wall of the Faithless. You've seen it. Then you know why.

choices.text: [INSIGHT DC 15] You're not a fanatic. You're grieving.
[INSIGHT DC 16] Something is using you. You're a door too, and you don't know it.
How many die for your crusade?
You're a murderer. I knew before the tea got cold.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: ...Her name was Maerin. Sixteen. She loved the world like it was a free gift from no one. When the Pox took her I followed her into the Fugue to watch my god be merciful — and watched Him wall her in instead. You're the first to see the father before the heretic. I won't forget that.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text:
...What a strange thing to say. The Crown of Horns is a tool. A means. I've held it in my mind for twenty years.
(But something flickers behind his eyes, and it is not entirely his own.)
...We'll speak of it again. When you've seen more.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: Don't try to read me like a chapbook, friend. Better minds have. Drink your tea.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: Thousands. I have counted every one and I will answer for them. Against an *eternity* of the Faithless dissolving into nothing, it is the smallest price ever asked for the largest mercy. You think me a monster. I think me *late.*

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: I'm not asking for your loyalty — I don't trust loyalty bought cheap. Just your curiosity. Walk with me a while. Decide for yourself whether I'm a monster. If you decide I am — you'll know exactly where to find me.

choices.text: I'll walk with you. For now.
I'll decide. But I'm watching you.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: Good. The tea's getting cold, and the road's long. We'll make a heretic of you yet — or you'll make an honest man of me. Either would be a mercy.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: Watching is wiser than most manage. Watch closely. I've nothing to hide — only a great deal to answer for.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: Yes. I am. I keep the count the way I once kept the names of the buried. But ask yourself, on your way to whoever you'll tell: every child in that Wall was put there by someone who also thought they were doing the smaller evil. Go with my blessing. I mean that. And my count.

nodes.speaker: Hensley, dying
nodes.text: An old man, hours from the end, grips your hand with a strength that surprises you. "You've an honest face. Tell me true — did my boy ever ask after me? Tomas. I drove him off, forty years gone, and I'd give the rest of my breath to know he... that he didn't hate me at the end." (Mother Cass, behind you, gives the smallest shake of her head. Tomas never came. Tomas never wrote. There is no record of mercy here.)

choices.text: "He asked after you. He forgave you. He told me so." (A lie, to ease him out.)
"I won't lie to a dying man. He never came. But you *asked* — that counts."

nodes.speaker: Hensley, dying
nodes.text: Something in his face comes untied — forty years of held breath, let go. "He did. Of course he did. He was always the better of us." He is asleep before you finish, smiling. You will never know if it was the cruelest kindness or the kindest cruelty. Mother Cass squeezes your shoulder. She has made this choice a thousand times. She does not tell you which way.

nodes.speaker: Hensley, dying
nodes.text: He weeps, which you feared. But then — quieter — "...I *asked,* though. After all this. That's not nothing, is it. To still be the kind of man who'd want to know." He holds that, instead of a comfort that wasn't his. It is a harder thing to die holding. He dies holding it anyway, and it is *his.*

choices.text: Who do you keep here?
What do you need?

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: Funny. I've cracked mansions, a palace, a god's own counting-house. But this — a room full of people the world forgot — this is the only kind of place I've ever walked into and felt my shoulders come *down.* I grew up in a room like this. Might've died in one, if the Harpers hadn't picked me out of it.

choices.text: You're not that kid anymore.
These are still your people.

nodes.speaker: Herald of the Gate
nodes.text: Returned. I felt you cross back — like a bell rung in an empty cathedral. Aldric Morn would speak with you. But the Doomguides have sealed the Cinderhaunt below, where the harvest still festers. Clear it, and the road to him opens.

choices.text: Why should I trust Aldric Morn?
I'll clear the Cinderhaunt.

nodes.speaker: Herald of the Gate
nodes.text: Trust nothing. But you've seen the Wall of the Faithless. You know the question he asks is real. Decide below — in blood and shadow, where such things are decided.

nodes.speaker: Herald of the Gate
nodes.text: Descend when you're ready; the stairs are marked. Try not to die a second time.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: So you're the one who walked back out of death. Relax — I'm a friend. The kind that knows every alley, lock, and lie in this city. The Harpers sent me to keep an eye on you. I'd rather keep the rest of me on you too. Mind some company down those stairs?

choices.text: A Harper spy. Why would I trust you?
Welcome aboard. We could use the blades — and the lockpicks.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: You don't. That's the smart play. But the Doomguides want you in a cell and Aldric wants you on an altar — and I want neither. That puts me a cut above your other options. Coming?

choices.text: Fair enough. Join me.
Not yet.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: Then I'm yours. Try not to get us both killed — I've grown fond of breathing.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: Suit yourself. I'll be here, looking trustworthy. Come find me when you change your mind.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: Returned, are you? A soul with a hole in it. Charming. I'm Varra — I've a hole in mine too, shaped exactly like a contract I signed when I was six. Misery loves company, and company with a sword is even better. Take me along before my patron remembers I exist?

choices.text: Welcome aboard. We've both got debts to outrun.
A pact-bound warlock sounds like a liability.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: Oh, absolutely. I'm a leash with a person on the end of it, and one day someone's going to *pull.* But until then I'm the funniest liability you'll ever meet, and I throw fire. Your call.

choices.text: Fair. Come on, then.
Not yet.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: Excellent. Don't get attached — everyone who buys me eventually gets a bill. But the interim's a riot.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: Mm. Hovering near the exit. Smart. I'd do the same, if I had one.

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: You pray to nothing, thief. I have watched you do it — that small nod, before a lock. To whom?

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: It's not a prayer. It's a promise. "Be quick, be quiet, be gone." Three gods I can keep.

choices.text: "He has a point, Garrow."
"He's stalling, and so are you."
[INSIGHT 13] "You nod to the people you've robbed. An apology you'll never say out loud."

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: Hm. Three gods you can keep. That is two more than most priests manage. Mind your footing.

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: ...Kelemvor keeps promises too. He keeps all of them. See that yours stay the harmless kind.

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: Stalling is a profession, grave-digger. You should try it. You'd live longer.

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: *(He doesn't look at you for a moment.)* ...That's a nasty little trick, reading a man like that. Do it again and I'll teach you a lock you can't pick. But — yes. Something like that.

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: It's a nervous habit. My old mentor had it. Leave the dead their habits, yes?

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: You flinch when I light the fire, Netherese. Every night. A wizard, flinching at a campfire.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: I watched a city the size of a sky fall burning for a day and a night. Karsus reached for godhood and the Weave answered with ruin. You light a campfire; I see Netheril. It is not the same fire. It is always the same fire.

choices.text: "Varra. Light it further from her."
"You both lost a home to your own ambition. Sit with that."
[ARCANA 15] "Karsus didn't fall to fire. He fell to a single missing word. You're not afraid of burning. You're afraid of being one word from ruin yourself."

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(She moves the kindling, grumbling.)* There. Happy? I'm not a monster. A monster wouldn't have asked.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(Varra's grin goes flat.)* ...We are not the same, conjurer. My ambition was a nation's. Yours fit in a contract.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(A long silence.)* ...You read me like Netherese script, and you are not even of the empire. Yes. One word. I have spent three centuries making certain I am never again one word from the end. *(quietly)* Thank you. No one has ever named it.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: Mind the Weave you cannot see, traveller. That is all I will say of it.

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: Sister — you knew my father. Before. When the Doomguides still said his name like a blessing.

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: I knew Aldric Morn. I buried beside him. He was the best of us, child, which is why the worst of him frightens me so. You have his eyes. Try not to have the rest.

choices.text: "She's not him, Garrow."
"Maerin, you don't have to be either of them."
[INSIGHT 14] "You're not warning her, Garrow. You're warning yourself — that you could still love him."

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: No. She is not. *(softer)* Forgive an old priest her caution, Maerin. I have watched good people become reasons. I would spare you that.

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: *(She smiles — the unguarded one Aldric describes when he can't sleep.)* I loved the world like it was a free gift from no one. He taught me that, before the Wall. I'd like to keep the half of him that's worth keeping.

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: *(She is quiet a long moment.)* ...You see too much, Returned. Yes. I have stood at a great many gravesides certain of the doctrine. I have never once stood at his and stayed certain. Leave an old woman that.

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: Mind the watch, both of you. The dead keep no schedule, and neither do his Choir.

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: You and I are the same animal, warlock. We both signed something we couldn't read all the way down.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(She laughs, no warmth in it.)* You signed a guild ledger, thief. I signed in a language with no word for "out." Don't you dare put us in the same sentence.

choices.text: "He's trying to say he understands."
"She's right, Roen. Some debts you can't pick the lock on."
"Both of you are still here. That's the part that matters."

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(A pause.)* ...Fine. He's trying. Badly, but trying. *(to Roen)* You want to understand, sticky-fingers? Then help me find the loophole. Mine, or yours. I'm not picky tonight.

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: *(He nods, slowly.)* Aye. I always thought a clever enough lock could open anything. Then I met a thing that was the lock and the key and the door. Point taken.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(She studies you, then snorts.)* ...Sentiment. From you. The world really is ending. Yes. We're both still here. Drinks are on the thief.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: You speak of Netheril as a wound, arcanist. To me it is recent news. I watched the Crown Wars; your floating cities were a child's bright idea built long after.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: Then you understand what no one else in this party can. The loneliness is not in the losing. It is in being the last shelf in the library no one else can read.

choices.text: "You're not the last shelf. You have each other now."
[HISTORY 16] "You're both wrong about why it hurts. It isn't that no one remembers. It's that you do."

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: *(The ancient elf inclines his head.)* A young thought. *(but not unkind)* ...And yet. Two unreadable shelves, side by side, make a small and stubborn library. I find I do not mind it.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(She and Ilfaeril share a look older than the others can follow.)* ...The traveller has it. Memory is the curse. The forgotten do not grieve. We are the ones who carry the empire's funeral, every day, alone — *(she glances at Ilfaeril)* — or, it seems, not entirely alone.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: A kind effort. The young always try to fix what only outlasts. Walk on, friend; the road is long, and I have time.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: You spared the Returned at the shrine. Knocked them cold instead of finishing them. Sentiment, grave-digger? From Kelemvor's own?

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: Mercy is not sentiment, tiefling. It is the discipline of not becoming the thing you fight. The Wall was built by people very certain they were doing the smaller evil. I will not earn my place in it.

choices.text: "She spared them because they were people once. That's all."
"Careful, Garrow. Mercy you can't afford is just a slower kind of cruelty."

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: Just so. Every soul in that Wall was a person once, dissolving for the crime of doubt. I bury people. I do not unmake them. Not even the ones that try to unmake me.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: *(Varra actually nods, surprised.)* ...Huh. The grey priest has teeth. She's right, Garrow — spare a wolf today, bury a shepherd tomorrow. Keep that in the ledger.

nodes.speaker: Garrow
nodes.text: Mind the dark, both of you. The Returned do not announce themselves. Mercy is for after the danger, never instead of sense.

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: Naeve — you've lived three hundred years. Does it stop hurting? Coming back, I mean. Remembering the Wall when the others are laughing.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(She considers the girl with something gentler than she shows the others.)* No. It does not stop. But it changes shape. The grief becomes a room you learn to live beside, rather than inside. You will furnish it. One day you will even invite people in.

choices.text: "That's the kindest thing I've heard you say."
"Maerin — you don't have to do it alone. None of us came back whole."

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: *(A faint, real smile.)* Do not tell Varra. I have a reputation as a cold thing, and it keeps people a useful distance away. *(beat)* ...You may stay inside it, though, little Returned. You and I keep the same room.

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: *(She breathes out, steadier.)* ...Thank you. Both of you. My father always said I loved the world like a free gift. I forgot, for a while, in the dark. I'd like to remember.

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: You make me twitchy, old one. Ten thousand years and you never lie. A thief can't read a man who's got nothing to hide.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: *(A slow, weathered smile.)* I had ten thousand years to spend every lie I owned, thief. Honesty is simply what is left when one runs out of other currency. You will arrive there too, given time.

choices.text: "He won't lie because there's no one left to lie to. That's not virtue. That's loss."
"Give it a rest, Roen. Not everyone's running a game."
[PERSUADE 14] "Then tell me one true thing you've never told anyone."

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: *(The old elf does not flinch.)* ...A sharp grief, well aimed. Yes. There is that, too. Honesty is cheap when the audience is gone. But it is still honesty, and I find I prefer it to the alternative I practiced for an age.

nodes.speaker: Roen
nodes.text: *(He raises his hands.)* Easy, easy. Just professional curiosity. A man who can't be read is the most interesting thing in any room.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: *(He is quiet for the length of three breaths.)* ...I have forgotten her name. The one I did the unforgivable thing for. Ten thousand years, and the face stays, and the name is gone. I have never said that aloud. *(to you)* There. One true thing. Spend it kindly.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: Some truths I have carried too long to set down for a parlor trick, thief. Ask me again when you mean it.

You fall further back than you have ever fallen — ten thousand years. Silver spires. Elven banners. A high court mid-session, beautiful and cold. And the matter before the court is not land, nor crowns. It is the *souls* of a defeated people.

nodes.speaker: The Returned
nodes.text: This is it. The first verdict. The afternoon they *decided* that the godless dead deserve to be unmade — before it hardened into the law of the Wall. I'm standing at the source.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: You carry the chill of the Wall on you, traveller — and the look of someone unstuck from time. I am Ilfaeril. I sit in that court. And I am about to do a thing I will spend ten thousand years trying to undo. If you have come to stop it... I would not stop you.

choices.text: What thing?
Then help me. Walk with me.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: I am about to raise my hand to damn an enemy people's souls to nothing — so that not even their memory has anywhere to rest. The court calls it justice. I will call it, in time, the only sin I could never bury. Come. Perhaps a soul from the future can do what my conscience cannot.

choices.text: Then let's change the vote. With me.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: Then my sword and my long, long regret are yours. Let us see if the past can be argued with.

nodes.speaker: High Lord Aelryth
nodes.text: You are not of this court, outsider, yet Ilfaeril vouches for you, so I will hear you. The matter stands: shall the godless dead of our fallen enemies be *unmade* — denied even a resting-place — that our victory be total? Speak. Why should mercy stay my hand?

choices.text: [PERSUADE DC 15] A law of vengeance outlives the vengeful. You damn your own descendants.
[INSIGHT DC 15] You do not want this. You want to be *told* you mustn't.
Their souls are yours to take. Take them.
(Say nothing. Let the court decide.)

nodes.speaker: High Lord Aelryth
nodes.text: ...You are right. Gods help me, you are right, and I have wanted someone to say it since the first banner fell. The motion is *withdrawn.* Let their dead rest. Let this one court, at least, be remembered for the cruelty it did NOT do.

nodes.speaker: High Lord Aelryth
nodes.text: Then it is done. Their dead are unmade, root and memory. ...The chamber feels colder than I expected. History will not record this hour. History will only inherit it.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: I have stood in this hall ten thousand times, in nightmares. I did not think I would ever stand in it again with *feet.* There — the third bench from the dais. That is where I raised my hand. I can smell the lamp-oil. Gods help me, I can *smell* it.

choices.text: Then change it. Argue the verdict down — you have the chance now.
Just breathe. I'm here. You don't face it alone this time.

nodes.speaker: Ferryman
nodes.text: "Help — please! My skiff's going under by the third piling and old Pell's still aboard, can't swim a stroke! The Fist won't come for a pauper and I can't haul him alone — *please!*"

choices.text: [Jump in.] Grab him — I've got the other side.
I've no time for this. (Walk on.)

nodes.speaker: Ferryman
nodes.text: You go in cold and graceless and get a fist in Pell's collar; between you the old man comes over the gunwale coughing river. The ferryman can't speak for shaking. The dockfolk saw. They always see who comes for the people the city writes off.

nodes.speaker: Ferryman
nodes.text: "...Aye. Of course. No one ever has." He turns back to the water alone. (Behind you, as you go, a splash and a younger voice swearing — someone else jumped. The Lower City keeps its own.)

nodes.speaker: A Grey Gravedigger
nodes.text: Mind the rows, traveller — fresh ones, all of them. When the gods walk as men and die as men, the dead come faster than the prayers. I bury the claimed on the hill. The *unclaimed* — the godless, the faithless — I bury here, in the low ground, where the law says their souls go to the Wall and nowhere. You have the look of someone who has argued with that law before.

choices.text: Once. Ten thousand years ago.

nodes.speaker: A Half-Unmade Voice
nodes.text: You can *see* me? Most can't, in the blue. I'm coming apart — not dying, undoing, like a word scratched out mid-sentence. The fire doesn't burn here so much as it *forgets* you. And yet you look at me and I stay. Stay a moment longer. Being looked at is the only thing keeping me a *me.*

choices.text: I see you. I'm not looking away.

nodes.speaker: A Keeper of the Bone Crown
nodes.text: Careful where you step, pilgrim — that is not a forge-fire's heat you feel. It is *attention.* They are beating a god's skull thin and curved, and the god is still *in* it — still awake, still choosing. Most who come here feel only the cold. You feel the *looking.* You've felt it before, haven't you. Somewhere far downriver of this hammer.

choices.text: I've felt it. On a gentle man's shelf.

nodes.speaker: A Keeper of the Bone Crown
Sister Garrow
nodes.text: Forty years a Doomguide and I never once let myself imagine *this.* They are beating a god of death into a crown of horns. Death itself — mortal, screaming, spent like coin. Every certainty I ever buried someone with is being forged into a tyrant's hat, right in front of me.

choices.text: What does it change, for you?
Steady. I'm here.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: Oh, I *love* it here. Feel that? The Weave's coming apart at the seams — every rule that ever held magic in place, just... optional, in the blue fire. My pact-flame's going feral in my hands and I have never felt more *free,* which should probably frighten me more than it does.

choices.text: Careful. Free isn't the same as safe.
What are you really thinking?

nodes.text: The grey is not a colour. It is the absence of the argument for colour. You are walking — though you don't remember choosing to — in a long grey river of the recently dead, toward a city that never gets closer. And then you see the Wall.

nodes.speaker: The Returned
nodes.text: It's made of *people.* Faces in the mortar. Mouths still moving — small fragments of names, of weather, of *someone please.* The godless dead, dissolving one unremembered syllable at a time. ...And near the end of the curve: a niche. Empty. The only empty one. With my name half-carved above it.

nodes.speaker: A girl in the Wall
nodes.text: You. You're the Returned — you can *reach* in here, I can feel it. My father's burning the whole world to pull me out of this. Don't you do it too. Whatever it costs — and it costs something, the Wall never gives without taking — I am not worth a—

choices.text: I'm pulling you free. Hold on to me.
...You're right. I won't pay that price.

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text:
—No. NO. The Wall — it's screaming, it won't give without taking, it's *taking someone* — I can feel the grey close on one of them — I'm sorry, I'm sorry, I didn't ask for this—

(She comes free in your arms, whole and luminous and fading and *furious.* And there are fewer of you than there were a moment ago. There will be fewer of you forever.)

nodes.speaker: Maerin
nodes.text: ...Good. You're learning. Most don't. They look at a girl in a wall and stop doing arithmetic. Go. Before the grey decides it likes the look of you, too.

choices.text: There's an empty niche with my name. Why?
Can anyone ever leave this Wall?

nodes.speaker: A Faithless soul
nodes.text: Because it has worn your name since before the world was young, and it is patient. The only way a soul comes *out* of this Wall is if another goes *in.* That is the oldest arithmetic there is. If you ever pull someone free of the grey — and I think you will; it's written all over you — know what the Wall asks back.

choices.text: Who would it take?

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: Grey stone, grey light, grey robes. Home — or what I called it for thirty years. The writ says heresy. Three counts: that I did not lay Aldric when the church bid me forget him. That I have voiced doubt of the Doom. And that I walk beside *you* — a soul no god claimed, which to them is a wound that walks.

choices.text: Doubt isn't heresy. It's the only honest prayer there is.
Then we leave. You owe a rigged court nothing.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: My father said the same, with a spade in his hand and no god in the conversation. The church disagrees. To the Doom, certainty *is* the sacrament — and I have spent my whole life being correct, and am no longer sure correct and good are the same word.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: No. I came here to answer, and I will. Not because I fear the verdict — because if I run, I never learn whether the thing I served for thirty years is as monstrous as I've begun to fear. The Justiciar inside is named Veld. He taught my catechism. Whatever he says in there, stay at my shoulder. I think today I find out what I believe.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Veld
nodes.text: Edda Garrow. You were the cleanest catechism I ever taught — a girl who buried the Faithless without weeping, because she understood the Wall was *just.* And here you stand reeking of compassion, with a creature beside you that the Wall itself spat back up. Kneel. Recant the doubt, disavow the Returned, and the Doom is merciful: you keep the grey.

choices.text: You weighted the scales before she walked in.
Ask her what she believes — don't tell her.

nodes.speaker: Justiciar Veld
nodes.text: Belief is not asked of a Doomguide; it is *issued.* The verdict is recorded already — it was recorded the day she chose your company over her vows. This trial is a courtesy, that she might kneel before she is broken to it. Sister. Last time. Renounce the Faithless thing, or be named one yourself.

choices.text: (Stand at her shoulder. Let her refuse with steel.)

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: Thirty years I weighed the dead by your feather, Veld, and called the trembling in my hands *reverence.* It was grief. I will not kneel to a verdict that was written before the question. If that makes me Faithless — then draw, and we will see whose god shows up for the fight.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text:
Veld's guard is down and the tribunal is scattered, but the temple still stands, and so does the record. They'll want my answer entered before I leave — recanted, or refused. The grey isn't a coat I can just drop; it's thirty years.

So say it with me. What do I answer the Scales?

choices.text: Recant. Keep the grey — do the work from inside. (save her standing)
Walk away from the faith entirely. Lay the dead under no one's law. (her freedom)
[Religion] Put Kelemvor's own doctrine on trial — the Faithless judgment is the heresy.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text:
...I recant. The words taste like the order to forget Aldric did. (She kneels, and signs, and the feather pan settles smug.)

They keep their Doomguide. I keep the only thing that ever mattered — the right to be at the bedside when the priests won't come. It is a smaller faith now, and a harder one, and I will carry it brittle for the rest of my days. But I carry it. You stayed. I'll not forget that you stayed.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text:
Then enter it: *Faithless, and unafraid.* (She unpins the grey clasp and sets it on the empty soul-pan, and walks out from under thirty years.)

Gods keep ledgers. So will I — my own. I'll lay the dead the way my father did, plainly, for anyone, under no law but the one in my own two hands. I should be terrified. Instead I feel like a held breath finally let go. Thank you — for being the proof that a soul no god claims can still be *good.*

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text:
*Read your own canon, Veld.* The Doom is not 'damn the unclaimed' — it is **'none shall pass unjudged.'** The Wall doesn't weigh the Faithless. It *discards* them, unweighed, ungrieved. That isn't the Doom's law. That's the church refusing to do its work — and calling its laziness scripture.

The heresy in this room was never my doubt. It's a faith that stopped reading. I don't leave the Doom and I don't kneel to its cowards. I take it *back.* From here — souls get weighed. Even the ones no god wanted. *Especially* those.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text:
Don't — don't hand me a clever reading of scripture to hide behind. I've hidden in correct answers my whole life; that's the sin that brought me here. There's no doctrine that makes this painless. There's only what I choose.

So plainly: do I kneel and keep the grey, or do I set it down and walk?

choices.text: Keep the grey. Do the work from inside. (save her standing)
Set it down. Walk free. (her freedom)

nodes.speaker: Harper Handler
nodes.text: A woman sits bound to a chair, jaw set, eyes flicking. "Caught her selling our drop-points to the Flaming Fist. Roen vouches for your judgement, so — your call. We can *turn* her, lean hard till she spies for us instead. Or you can argue for cutting her loose. I'll warn you: loose, she might run straight back to the Fist. People mostly do."

choices.text: Turn her. A frightened asset is still an asset.
Cut her loose. Give her the choice no one's given her.

nodes.speaker: Harper Handler
nodes.text: It works, the way leaning on the frightened works. She'll spy for you now, and hate you, and be useful, and the work will get done. The handler nods, satisfied. You watch the woman's face close like a door and understand, a little better than you wanted to, how the Wall got built one reasonable decision at a time.

nodes.speaker: Harper Handler
nodes.text: The handler grimaces but cuts the ropes — Roen's name carries weight. The woman rubs her wrists, stares at you like you've handed her a live coal, and walks out into the dark unowned by anyone for the first time in years. Maybe she runs back to the Fist. Maybe she doesn't. You gave her the one thing the Wall never gives: the dignity of being allowed to decide.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: I knew this would find me eventually. Ten thousand years is a long time for a thing to wait, but patience is the one virtue the dead have in surplus. This reliquary belonged to Maerith of the Singing Vale — one of the people my court voted to *unmake.* Not kill. Unmake. We denied her dead even a grave to rest in. I raised my hand. I have never once raised it back down.

choices.text: You were one vote. The Law took thousands.
You don't have to open it. Not today.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: Do not do that. Do not make it smaller to spare me. A thousand hands built the Wall, yes — and every one of them told itself it was only one hand. That arithmetic *is* the atrocity. I will not hide inside it. The name on this lock is hers, and I am the last creature alive who can still say it. That is not absolution. It is a debt with my name on the invoice.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: There is no 'not today' left in me, friend. I have guided ten thousand strangers gently past the Wall and never once dared face the single soul I owed. If I walk away from this, I am only what I have always been: a man performing mercy on everyone except the people he wronged. No. I open it.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: ...But we are not alone in this crypt. That cold, underneath the old cold — that is the Choir. Of course they came. To them I am not a sinner; I am a *relic* — the first witness, the proof their unmaking is holy. They will want what's in that reliquary, and they will want me. Draw. They do not get to make my worst day into their scripture.

nodes.speaker: The Pale Cantor
nodes.text: Witness. *Witness.* Do you feel how the air leans toward you? Ten thousand years you have knelt at the edge of the Wall begging to be forgiven, and never once understood: you do not need forgiveness. You need only to be *right.* You voted to unmake the unclaimed. The Unmade simply agrees with you. Give us the relic, and we will give you the one mercy the gods never did — we will tell you that you were correct.

choices.text: He's worth more than the worst thing he ever did.
(To Ilfaeril) Don't take the comfortable lie.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: You offer to end my guilt by proving I never should have had any. (He sets himself between the Cantor and the reliquary.) Cantor — that is not mercy. That is the most obscene thing anyone has said to me in ten thousand years. My guilt is the only monument those people have. I will not let you melt it down for a key.

choices.text: (Stand with him. Drive the Choir off.)

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text: Maerith. I am sorry. I have brought a war to your door again. (He lifts his shield, and for the first time in an age it does not feel like a burden.) Let us earn the right to open it.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text:
The Choir is scattered, and the reliquary is quiet, and it knows my voice. It is opening.

(Inside: no weapon, no relic of power. A message-stone, set ten thousand years ago by a woman who knew that one day her judge might be the only one left to hear it. Maerith's voice fills the crypt — and it is not cursing him. It is *forgiving* him.)

I... I do not know what to do with this. I prepared for her hate. I have no defense against her mercy. Tell me. What do I do?

choices.text: Refuse it. Keep paying — guide the lost, forever. (the penance)
Accept it. You're allowed to be more than your worst vote. (be forgiven)
[Insight] She isn't closing your debt. She's handing you a charge.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text:
No. I cannot take it — not yet, perhaps not ever. To accept her forgiveness is to set down the weight, and the weight is the last true thing I carry of her and her people. So I will keep it. I will guide the lost past the Wall until the stars go out, and call that my answer.

(He bows his head. It is devotion, and it is also a kind of hiding, and he half knows it.) Thank you for standing with me. Even a man who refuses rest is glad not to refuse it alone.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text:
...All right. (The word costs him more than any wound the Choir dealt.) All right, Maerith. I accept it. I accept that I am not only the hand I raised — that ten thousand years of trying to make it right is allowed, at last, to *count.*

(He breathes out, and something ancient and rigid in him eases.) I had forgotten that forgiveness is a thing you do for the *living.* She freed me to stop. One day — not today, but one day — I think I might even rest. You gave me that. I will not waste it.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text:
Wait — listen to her words again. She does not say *be at peace.* She says *do not let it happen to anyone else.* She is not closing the account. She is *hiring* me.

Gods. Ten thousand years I treated her forgiveness as a door I had to be worthy to walk through — and it was a *sword* she was holding out, hilt-first, the whole time. I accept it. Not as an ending. As a commission. The Wall took her people; I will spend whatever I have left tearing a hole in it for the rest of them. That is the only forgiveness worth the name: the kind that puts you back to work. You didn't absolve me, friend. You *armed* me.

nodes.speaker: Ilfaeril
nodes.text:
Do not reach for a clever reading of her words to make this easy on me. I have hidden inside elegant interpretations for ten thousand years; it is its own kind of cowardice. Her meaning is plain enough. The only question is mine to answer.

So say it simply: do I take what she offers, or do I keep paying?

choices.text: Keep paying. Guide the lost, forever. (the penance)
Take what she offers. You're allowed to stop. (be forgiven)

nodes.speaker: Flaming Fist
nodes.text: "Caught this one's hand in a fishwife's purse. Edict says a finger for a first theft, or the Seatower cells." The child — eight, maybe nine, all elbows and terror — doesn't even struggle. They've done this before. "Unless someone wants to pay the fishwife's loss and stand surety. You someone, Returned?"

choices.text: [Stand surety.] I'll cover it. Let the child go.
The law's the law. (Let them take the child.)

nodes.speaker: Flaming Fist
nodes.text: The picket shrugs, takes your coin, and lets go of a fistful of ragged tunic. The child is gone before you can speak — but at the mouth of the alley they stop, and look back at you, and do not run. They are *deciding something.* In the Lower City, that is how every good thing starts: a child, surprised by mercy, deciding the world might hold some after all.

nodes.speaker: Flaming Fist
nodes.text: "Sensible." He marches the child off toward the Seatower. They don't cry. That's the part that stays with you — not tears, just the small flat face of someone learning, on schedule, exactly what the city thinks they're worth. The fishwife won't meet your eye. She has children too.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: Do you hear it? No — of course you don't. It's the resonance of a live mythallar, and there has not been one of those in this world for a thousand years. This is a piece of the Seventh Enclave. My Enclave. It never finished falling. It has been *up here,* humming my home's exact note, the whole time I thought I'd lost it.

choices.text: Then let's go in. Carefully.
How is anything still aloft after the Weave died?

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: Carefully. Yes. I have not been careful with anything I loved since I was old enough to derive a proof, and look what that bought the sky. So — carefully. Stay close. The wards here knew my hand once. I do not know if a thousand years remembers.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: It shouldn't be. That's what frightens me. A mythallar that loses its Weave goes dark and the city comes down — I *watched* it, all of them, at once. For this one to still be burning, someone left it a reserve. A calculation precise enough to ration a thousand years of magic down to a candle-flame. There were perhaps three people alive who could do that math. I was one.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: So either a colleague I loved spent their last breath saving a rock — or I did, and forgot, and have spent a millennium grieving a thing that was waiting for me to come back. I have to know which. Whatever's at the core, I face it. Come.

nodes.speaker: Vaelin (an echo)
nodes.text: Arcanist Naeve. You took your time. I am — was — Vaelin, Steward of the Seventh. What's left of me the mythallar keeps, the way it keeps everything: imperfectly, and forever. You want to know who saved this shard. Child. *You* did. You set the rationing wardstone in the last hour, while the rest of us screamed at Karsus's sky.

choices.text: I don't remember doing that.
Then why does it feel like a tomb, not a rescue?

nodes.speaker: Vaelin (an echo)
nodes.text: Because it is both. You saved a fragment by sentencing it to fall *slowly* — for a thousand years, instead of an afternoon. Everyone in it is still here. Still falling. Still mine to steward. And the candle is nearly out: the wardstone has been drinking the world's last live Weave to keep us, and there is almost none left. You came back at the very end of the math.

nodes.speaker: Vaelin (an echo)
nodes.text: There is one mercy left to me: the core's old protocol. *Admit no one who would unmake the work.* I cannot tell anymore whether you have come to save us or to finish us — and the wards will not let either of us decide it with words. They are waking. I am sorry, Arcanist. Get to the core, and *then* choose. I find I would rather lose to you than to time.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text:
The wards are still. The core is open, and Vaelin's right — the wardstone is nearly dry. Whatever I do, I do it now, in the last live Weave my own younger hand set aside.

Three ways this ends. I have run them a thousand times in my sleep and never once been brave enough to pick. Help me. Please.

choices.text: Lock it in stasis. Keep the last of Netheril, whole, forever. (preserve)
Let it finish falling. Lay your world to rest — and carry it. (release)
[Arcana] The wardstone can give its Weave back — not embalm the dead, but feed the living.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text:
...I can't. I can't be the hand that ends it twice. (She seals the wardstone, and the shard stops falling — a snowflake caught mid-air, forever.)

They're safe now. Frozen, but safe; mine to keep. Don't say it. I know what it is — a tomb I'll visit and call a home, a mistake I refused to carry because clutching it was easier. I am not ready to let the sky finish. Maybe I never will be. But you stood here while I wasn't brave. That is its own kind of saving.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text:
No. A thing held out of its ending isn't saved — it's only made to fall forever, and I have asked enough people to fall forever. (She lifts her hand from the wardstone, and gently, the last of Netheril lets go.)

It's done. It fell. It was always going to fall, and I have spent a thousand years pretending my grief was a calculation I could redo. It isn't. Some mistakes you don't erase. You *carry* them. I think — I think I can carry this now. Because I'm not carrying it alone.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text:
Wait — *wait.* The wardstone isn't a cage, it's a held breath. The Weave inside it isn't spent, it's *saved* — the last live magic in the world, and I have been treating it as a shroud. I can open my own hand. Let it go not down, but *out* — back into the dead Weave of the present, a seed instead of a tomb.

There's a notation under my old proof in a hand I almost know — it says the same thing, as if someone left me the answer to find. Netheril doesn't get to live again. But it gets to *give.* My people end as the thing that makes new magic possible. That's not erasing the mistake. That's the mistake, finally, building something.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text:
Don't — don't hand me an elegant third door. Elegance is what I trusted last time, and it dropped a civilization out of the sky. There's no clever proof that makes this not hurt. There's only what I'm willing to carry.

So, plainly: do I freeze them and keep the tomb, or do I let the sky finish falling?

choices.text: Keep them. Freeze the shard. (preserve)
Let it fall. Lay it to rest. (release)

nodes.text: You fall out of your own century. Gold light. Sunlight on glass. A city — an entire city — floating in an impossible blue sky, held aloft by magic so total it has forgotten it is magic at all.

And then — all at once, everywhere, like a bell rung underwater — every spell in the world goes silent. The light dies. And the city, which flies by magic, begins, very slowly and then all at once, to FALL.

nodes.speaker: The Returned
nodes.text: Karsus's Folly. The day the goddess of magic died. I'm standing inside the first apocalypse — and the floor is going out from under the world. Move. MOVE.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: You pulled me from the rubble. Impossible — the ward-stones should have rendered me to ash. Unless... you are not of this Now at all. You have the look of someone *falling through time.* I have read of people like you. In the margins of a forbidden proof. In a hand identical to my own — written centuries before I was born.

choices.text: Whose hand?
No time. The city's falling — come with me, NOW.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: Yours, I rather think. Which should be impossible, and is the single most interesting thing that has ever happened to me, and we are both about to die discussing it. Get me off this dying enclave and I will tell you everything I have read about you. About what you are.

choices.text: Deal. Stay close.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: Then I am yours, impossible one. Mind the gaps in the floor — gravity has only just remembered it has opinions. Let us not be where the marble is not.

nodes.speaker: Naeve
nodes.text: I have done the impossible arithmetic of my own life ten thousand times, and never once did it let me back *here* — the hour it fell, the air still warm with the Weave before Karsus reached too far. That balcony. I derived my first proof on that balcony. It is about to be sky.

choices.text: We can't stop the fall. But you're not watching it alone this time.
Is there anything here you need to see?

nodes.speaker: The Wall
nodes.text: *(There is no speaker. The voice is the architecture itself — a sound like a million held breaths.)* Here is the place kept for you. Read the niche. The name is half-formed; the mortar has not set. You were always going to be here. The only surprise is that you are reading it with eyes.

nodes.speaker: The Wall
nodes.text: *(Your own name is being spoken somewhere behind you, over a body that is yours — and one word in it you cannot hold. It slides off the mind like water off glass.)*

choices.text: "Whose name is that?"
[ARCANA 12] "This isn't a punishment. It's a filing system."
"I don't belong here."

nodes.speaker: The Wall
nodes.text: Yours. It has always been yours. The word you cannot keep is the one that makes you a person instead of a vacancy. Someone is saying it now. Someone will forget it. *(A long, patient silence.)* So will you.

nodes.speaker: The Wall
nodes.text: *(For the first time the voice seems almost to attend to you.)* ...A cold way to put it. A true one. We are the souls no ledger wanted. Filed under nothing. You read quickly, for the newly dead. *(beat)* You will need that.

nodes.speaker: The Wall
nodes.text: Doubt is the crime, little vacancy. You will learn the punishment by living inside it. Read your niche. Wait your turn.

nodes.speaker: The Wall
nodes.text: *(The held breath of a million almost laughs.)* No one belongs here. That is the entire point. Belonging was the thing you all lacked. *(The mortar at the edge of your name has not set.)* ...And yet yours is the one that did not take. Curious. Hold that thought. Something else has hold of it too.

nodes.speaker: The Wall
nodes.text: *(Far off, a hand on your true chest. A pull, like a hook behind the navel. The Wall recedes — unwilling, for the first time in its existence, to let one go.)*

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(Grey robes, a grave-warden's lantern, a face that has buried more people than it has loved.)* Be still. You stopped breathing on this slab a quarter-hour past. I closed your eyes myself. And now they are open, and Kelemvor's seal on your soul is — *(she falters, a priest losing her certainty)* — torn. Cleanly. From the inside.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: I have served the Lord of the Dead forty years. I have never once seen a thing come back. So I will ask plainly, and your answer decides whether I help you up or put you down: do you know what you are?

choices.text: "I saw the Wall. My name is in it."
"I don't know. But something pulled me back."
[INSIGHT 13] "You're not afraid of me. You're afraid you've been lied to for forty years."
"Put me down, then. If you can."

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(She goes very still.)* The Wall of the Faithless. You saw it from the inside and walked out the wrong way. *(She helps you sit, and her hands are shaking, and she is ashamed of it.)* Then the doctrine I have preached over a thousand graves is at best incomplete. I would rather know that than be comfortable. Come. The Cinderhaunt is no place to be newly alive.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: Something. *(dry)* In my experience, child, "something" with the strength to unmake Kelemvor's own seal is rarely benign. We will find out together what has its hand on you. On your feet. The harvesters will have heard your heart restart.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(The lantern trembles, just once.)* ...You read the living as easily as the dead read you. Yes. If you are real — and you are insultingly warm for a corpse — then someone has been wrong for a very long time, and I have helped them be wrong. I intend to make that right. Starting with getting you out of here. Move.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(A grim flicker that might, in better light, be respect.)* No. I don't think I can. And a thing I cannot put down is a thing I had best understand. Walk with me, Returned. The exit is through the harvest-floor, and the harvest is not finished.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: Spare me the cold-reading, corpse. Better confessors than you have tried. *(She offers a hand anyway.)* Up. Questions later. Knives now.

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: Stay behind me until you remember how your own legs work. When the Returned come — and they will — strike for the joints, not the heart. They have no more use for hearts than you did, an hour ago.

nodes.speaker: Choir Herald
nodes.text: *(A masked acolyte of the Faithless Choir, frozen at the cellar mouth, staring at you like a sum that won't add.)* No. No, you're — I sealed that door. I read the rites. You were *filed.* They don't — *(his voice cracks behind the mask)* — they never come back. Not once. Not in all the years.

nodes.speaker: Choir Herald
nodes.text: He'll want to know. Gods help me, he'll *want* you. *(He backs toward the stair, hands raised.)* I'm not your enemy. I'm not — we're trying to *end* the Wall, do you understand? The man who runs us watched it take his own daughter. If you are what you are, then you are the thing he has prayed twenty years to find.

choices.text: "Who is he?"
[PERSUADE 14] "Then take me to him. No tricks."
"I'm no one's miracle. Run."

nodes.speaker: Choir Herald
nodes.text: Aldric Morn. *(He says it the way some say a saint's name and others say a curse — and isn't sure which he means.)* The Heretic of the Lower City. Forty years a Doomguide, until he stopped believing the dead were being kept and started believing they were being *thrown away.* Find a chandler's shop with a kettle always on. He'll be expecting you. He's been expecting you for twenty years.

nodes.speaker: Choir Herald
nodes.text: *(He hesitates, then nods, fast.)* ...Yes. Yes, all right. But not like this, not bleeding, not with a Doomguide of Kelemvor at your shoulder — *(he glances at Garrow)* — she'll have us both in cells. Go up, go quiet, find the kettle. Tell him the herald sent you. Tell him... tell him it's true. After all this time, it's *true.*

nodes.speaker: Choir Herald
nodes.text: *(Something in him sags with relief and grief at once.)* ...Maybe you're right. Maybe a miracle should get to choose. *(He turns to flee, then stops.)* His name is Aldric Morn. If you ever want to know what you are — that's the man who's spent his life asking. For what it's worth. For whatever you are.

nodes.speaker: Choir Herald
nodes.text: *(He's already gone up the stair, three steps at a time, shouting a name into the dark above — Aldric, Aldric, it's true — and the word follows you out of the Cinderhaunt like a hook already set.)*

nodes.speaker: Sister Garrow
nodes.text: *(quietly, beside you)* Aldric Morn. I knew him, Returned. Before. He was the best of us. *(She looks at the stair the herald fled up, and her grave-warden's face is, for once, simply afraid.)* Which is exactly why I want to reach him before he reaches you.

nodes.speaker: Tally (a one-eyed storyteller)
nodes.text: —and the hero died in the first chapter and came back *wrong.* Oh! Speak of the margin. Sit. I've a pouch for you and a question. The pouch first; you'll want it for the question. I keep a little vault between the pages — ten pedestals, a brazier, and the best company you'll ever lose a riddle to. Care to play?

choices.text: Show me your Vault of Tens.
Who ARE you?
Maybe later.

nodes.speaker: Tally
nodes.text: A storyteller. A gambler. A note someone left in the margin of a very long book and forgot to erase. Which of those is true? *All of them, on a good day.* Now — the Vault?

choices.text: Lead on.
Not yet.

nodes.speaker: Tally
nodes.text: Wonderful. Mind the step between the pages — it's a long way down to the footnotes.

nodes.speaker: Tally
nodes.text: Mm. I'll be here. I'm always here. That's rather the whole tragedy of me.

nodes.speaker: Tally (between the pages)
nodes.text: Fancy meeting you here, time-walker. A quick one — no vault required: I'll not speak till I'm spoken to, and then I always have the last word. Mock me from a mountain, and you're mocked in your own voice, unheard. What am I?

choices.text: An echo.
A shadow.
Silence.

nodes.speaker: Tally
nodes.text: Mm. You'll fight a roomful of those, later — wearing the faces you love. Keep the answer. Keep the warning. I'll be three pages ahead, as ever.

nodes.speaker: Tally
nodes.text: Ha — no. But you *tried*, and trying amuses me almost as much as winning. Almost. Off you go.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text:
This is the safehouse the cipher named. Harper-warded, supposedly. But the wards are cold, the door's off the latch, and there's no countersign chalked on the step.

Someone I care about ran this house. I told you I had a person I'd pull from the Wall. I didn't tell you she's my sister. Wrenna. And I didn't tell you she's not in the Wall yet.

choices.text: Why lie about it?
Then let's get her out. Lead on.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: Because if you knew I had something to lose, you'd know exactly where to cut me. Habit. The Harpers train it into you young, and family trains it younger.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: Doomguide sign on the lintel — fresh. Kelemvor's people have been here, and they don't leave witnesses, only verdicts. Knives out. Quietly.

nodes.speaker: Wrenna Alleywind
nodes.text:
Roen. Gods — Roen, you have to go, now, before they— 

(She stops. Looks at you. Something in her face folds shut.) 

...You brought the Returned here. To my house. Do you have any idea what they'd give to have this one on an altar?

choices.text: "They." The Doomguides. You've been talking to them.
Wrenna — what did you do?

nodes.speaker: Wrenna Alleywind
nodes.text: They had a writ with your name on it, Roen. The Wall, for 'consorting with the Returned.' I had a choice: hand them Harper routes, names, drops — or watch my brother mortared into nothing while I lived. So I lied. To the Harpers, to you, to everyone. For two years.

choices.text: (Say nothing. The Doomguides are coming.)

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text: ...Two years. You sold the Harpers to keep me breathing, and let me think I owed *you* nothing. We'll have that fight later. Right now there are six Doomguides between her and the door. Decide who you are after we live.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text:
The cell's down. The safehouse is ours — what's left of it. And Wrenna's standing here a traitor to the Harpers and the only family I've got, in the same body. 

I can't make this call clean. Help me make it.

choices.text: She did it to save you. Take her home. (forgive)
She betrayed the Harpers. The rules exist for a reason. (condemn)
[Persuasion] Neither. We turn her two years of lies on the Doomguides.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text:
Yeah. Yeah — come here, you idiot. (He pulls her in.) We'll explain it to the Harpers together, and if they don't like it, I know every alley out of this city.

I won't forget that you let me choose mercy. Whatever's at the end of your road, Returned — I'm on it.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text:
(A long silence.) ...You're right. You're right, and I hate that you're right. She turns herself in. The Harpers get their routes back, their traitor, and a debt to us.

It's the correct call. Don't expect me to thank you for it. But here — everything the cell carried. Use it well.

nodes.speaker: Roen Alleywind
nodes.text:
...Gods. That could *work*. Wrenna's still on their books as an asset — they don't know the cell's dead. We feed them what *we* choose, and walk a knife straight into Kelemvor's people.

You didn't just save my sister. You turned her into the best weapon we've got. Remind me never to play cards against you.

nodes.speaker: Wrenna Alleywind
nodes.text:
It's a pretty idea. It's also how people like me get caught and people like him get a writ. No. You don't get to gamble my brother on your cleverness. 

Choose plainly, Returned: do I go home with him, or do I turn myself in?

choices.text: Take her home. (forgive)
She turns herself in. (condemn)

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: So. The invoice didn't lie. This is where I signed — or where the thing that signed *for* me did. I was six. I remember the candles and a man with a kind voice and too many teeth, and then I remember being able to throw fire and never being able to sleep.

choices.text: You were a child. A child can't sign anything.
Then let's tear the contract up. Together.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: Tell that to the Hells' clerks. Down there, intent is a technicality and signatures are scripture. A child can't sign — but a *broker* can sign on a child's behalf, for a cut. That's who's waiting in there. The one who sold me. I've wanted to meet him my whole life.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: His name's Quill. Don't let the smile fool you — everything in that chapel is a clause, including the floor. Whatever he offers you, the price is always in a footnote. Ready?

nodes.speaker: Quill
nodes.text: Varra! Look at you, all grown and overdue. And you've brought the Returned — oh, this is *delicious.* A soul no god will claim, standing next to a soul a Hell already owns. Do you know what the two of you are worth in the right ledger?

choices.text: She was six. You sold a child.
What do you actually want, Quill?

nodes.speaker: Quill
nodes.text: I want what I'm owed, and a promotion. The patron wants Varra's soul matured and collected. *I* want to renegotiate upward — and you, Returned, are the collateral that lets me. Sign her term over to me, take a boon, walk away clean. She was always going to pay. Why should you?

choices.text: (Refuse. Draw steel.)

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text: He always did talk like a contract reads. Here's my counter-offer, Quill: every fireball you ever gave me, all at once. Let's see which of us the footnotes favor.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text:
Quill's down, and the contract's still on the lectern — glowing, waiting for a hand. It can't just be torn; the Hells don't recognize destruction, only transfer. Someone has to be named as holder, or named as paid.

I've read it a hundred times in my nightmares. Three ways this ends. Help me pick.

choices.text: Name me the holder. I'll carry your debt. (take the burden)
Burn it with your own fire. Owe it yourself, freely. (her choice)
[Arcana] There's a third name in the fine print — the patron's own.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text:
...You'd put your name where mine is. After everyone who ever bought me sent a bill.

(For once she isn't joking.) I won't let it ride forever — we'll find the loophole, you and I, before any clerk comes knocking. But tonight? Tonight I sleep. First time in twenty years. Thank you, Returned.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text:
No. You don't get to be one more person who pays for me. This one's mine.

(She presses her palm to the page and it goes up in violet fire — her fire, freely spent.) Still owed. Still doomed. But by my own hand now, on my own clock. You can't imagine how light that is. I'm with you to the end — and I choose it.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text:
There — *there*, under the seventh seal. The patron countersigned in their true name to bind a child. Which means the contract binds *them* to the same court. Quill didn't sell me a debt. He sold me a leash with two collars.

We don't pay it. We *enforce* it — against them. Gods, Returned. You just made my patron my debtor.

nodes.speaker: Varra
nodes.text:
Don't. I see you chasing the clever exit, and the clever exit is how clever people end up as footnotes. There's no trick here. There's just who pays.

So say it plainly: do you carry it for me, or do I burn it myself?

choices.text: I'll carry it. (take the burden)
Burn it yourself, freely. (her choice)
